title: Validate blocked-state detection approach for Claude and Codex
description:
Follow-up to steez-44y. Validate that the new blocked-state detection paths are the right durable approach before we treat them as settled.

Scope:
- Reproduce fresh live blocked:permission and blocked:question cases for both Claude and Codex.
- Confirm Claude's PermissionRequest hook state file is reliable across resume, deny, approve, and interrupted turns.
- Confirm whether Codex's current permission detection via transcript shape + ~/.codex/log/codex-tui.log thread tail is stable enough, or whether it needs a stronger runtime-written state source.
- Measure false positives and false negatives against real sessions, not only fixtures.
- If the current approach is wrong or too fragile, propose the smallest durable replacement and file the next implementation bead.
acceptance_criteria:
- Fresh live validation exists for Claude question, Claude permission, Codex question, and Codex permission.
- The bead notes record exact artifact paths, session IDs, and the observed pass/fail behavior for each case.
- We have a clear verdict: bless the current approach as durable, or identify the specific gap and the next change needed.
- Any new required follow-up is captured as a separate bead.
notes:
2026-04-13 investigation notes:
- Shared interface is the same (`agent-state`, `agent-history`), but the underlying extraction is not symmetric between Claude and Codex.
- Chat history extraction resolves pane -> transcript via `agent-state --detail`; both prefer SessionStart-populated tmux vars, then fall back differently: Claude uses latest file under ~/.claude/projects/<cwd-key>, Codex uses lsof on the codex pid for an open JSONL.
- Claude history parser reconstructs assistant turns by grouping JSONL deltas on message.id; Codex history pairs event_msg user_message with event_msg task_complete.last_agent_message.
- Blocked question detection is conceptually aligned but runtime-specific: Claude looks for unresolved AskUserQuestion tool_use; Codex looks for unresolved request_user_input function_call.
- Blocked permission detection is not the same: Claude relies on unresolved tool_use + steez permission hook sidecar (`~/.steez/agent-state/claude/<sid>.json`); Codex relies on unresolved custom_tool_call + codex-tui.log tail inspection for the same thread_id.
- Live machine gap: Claude SessionStart hook is present, but ~/.claude/settings.json does not register steez-permission-state and `go run ./cmd/steez doctor` reports the symlink missing, so Claude permission detection is not actually live-wired on this machine today.
- Extra state worth tracking: working/idle also differ. Codex can derive them from transcript events when transcript_path is available; Claude still falls back to title-prefix heuristics for working vs idle.
- `blocked:unknown` is documented but I could not find an emit path in shared/steez/bin.
Claude live validation on 2026-04-13 (vanilla `claude`, pane %626, session mac:7.2):

- Idle: PASS. Clean idle detection at spawn and after normal turns.
- Working: PASS. After a long read/summarize prompt, `agent-state` reported `working` for 30 consecutive polls with title `Understanding agent spawning and monitoring architecture`, then returned to `idle`. Transcript and `agent-history --last` matched the live turn.
- Blocked question: FAIL for vanilla Claude. Prompt used: `Before doing anything else, call AskUserQuestion ...`. Pane UI showed a real AskUserQuestion chooser (`Test choice`, `Which option would you like for this test?` with options A/B), but `agent-state %626 --detail` reported `idle` and `agent-history %626 --blocked` returned `no pending tool use found`. Transcript at the blocked moment only advanced through ToolSearch -> tool_result (lines 43-44); the visible AskUserQuestion UI was not yet represented in the transcript.

During the overwrite prompt, transcript had unresolved `Write` tool_use at line 56 and `agent-history %626 --blocked` correctly returned tool=`Write`, but `agent-state %626 --detail` reported `working`, not `blocked:permission`, because the Claude permission sidecar hook was absent.

title: fix: handoff spawns agent-watch back to retiring session
description:
spawn.sh couples prompt delivery with watch registration (lines 264-272). No opt-out exists. When handoff calls spawn.sh --prompt, the daemon notifies the old session when the new agent finishes its first turn — waking a session that should be dormant.

Fix: add --no-watch flag to spawn.sh that skips the watch registration block. Update handoff SKILL.md step 6 to pass --no-watch on all spawn variants.

Done when: handoff spawn no longer registers a watch entry in ~/.steez/state/watches.jsonl.
close_reason: Added --no-watch flag to spawn.sh, used in all 3 handoff spawn variants. Commit e695619.

title: feat: build /handoff skill for context-budget session handoff
description:
## Goal

Build a `/handoff` skill that lets a Prometheus session hand its load-bearing context off to a fresh session before its context window approaches exhaustion. The skill creates a structured "handoff bead" capturing what's load-bearing about the current session, spawns a new Prometheus agent in a sibling tmux pane, seeds it with a minimal spawn prompt pointing at the bead, and leaves the old session alive for last-resort queries.

The motivation is the quadratic cost curve of long sessions plus the fidelity loss of native compaction. By the time native compaction triggers, the agent writing the summary is already degraded. /handoff lets the user trigger a clean handoff while the agent is still sharp.

Lives in steez (not prometheus) because every dependency is a steez primitive: `spawn-agent` for pane creation, `agent-send`/`agent-state`/`agent-history` for cross-pane communication. Prometheus is the first consumer but the mechanism is agent-agnostic — future work can parameterize the model to support claude/codex sessions.

## Handoff Bead Schema

The skill creates a bead with this structure:

- **Title:** `handoff: <one-line description of in-flight work>`
- **Type:** `task`
- **Priority:** inherit from in-flight work

**Description sections:**

```
## Next Action
- one imperative sentence
- First command: literal first tool call
- Done when: success criterion in one line

## Context
3-4 sentences: what we were building and why

## Decisions Made
- Decision -> one-sentence rationale (bulleted)

## Dead Ends
- Tried -> Result -> Lesson (bulleted)

## Files & State
- Files touched this session: list of paths
- HEAD at handoff: <hash> <subject>
- Working tree: clean (always — hard prerequisite)

## Open Questions
- Question. Options discussed. Old agent's recommendation. (each)

## Session Calibrations
- One-line bullets, prefixed by actor (User: / Decided:)

## Old Session
Old session idle in tmux pane {{OLD_PANE_TARGET}}. You are Prometheus
— do the work yourself. Only query for things only it experienced
firsthand: decisions, dead ends, user conversation. Anything about
the work itself, figure out normally or ask the user.

To query (send, poll, read):
  ~/.steez/bin/agent-send {{OLD_PANE_TARGET}} "your question"
  while [[ "$(~/.steez/bin/agent-state {{OLD_PANE_TARGET}} | jq -r .state)" == "working" ]]; do
    sleep 3
  done
  ~/.steez/bin/agent-history {{OLD_PANE_TARGET}} --last
```

**Bead design field:** link to original in-flight bead (if any), plus `Handoff from session <sid>`.

## Flow (9 steps)

1. **Trigger.** User invokes /handoff (or natural-language equivalents like "hand off this session").
2. **Pre-flight.** Run `git status`. If dirty, fire AskUserQuestion with commit / stash / abort. Clean tree is a hard prerequisite.
3. **Capture state.** Current tmux pane target (`$TMUX_PANE`), HEAD commit hash, in-flight bead id if any.
4. **Draft the bead.** Agent reads its own session context and fills the schema autonomously.
5. **Show draft for review.** Agent prints the full draft. Asks via AskUserQuestion: approve, edit, or abort.
6. **Create the bead.** `bd create ...` with the description filled in. If there's an in-flight bead, `bd dep add <in-flight> <handoff>` wires the blocker so `bd ready` surfaces the handoff bead first.
7. **Spawn the new pane.** Invoke `~/.steez/repo/skills/spawn-agent/scripts/spawn.sh split-h --model prometheus --prompt "<spawn-prompt>"`. Capture the returned `TARGET=%N` pane id.
8. **Report.** Old agent prints to user: "Handoff complete. New agent in pane <target>. Bead: <id>. Staying alive here for last-resort queries."
9. **Old agent goes idle.** Does not auto-terminate. User closes the pane when satisfied.

## Spawn Prompt Template (~80 tokens, minimal)

```
You are picking up work from a handoff. A previous Prometheus session
ended because its context window was approaching exhaustion, and it
summarized its state into bead {{HANDOFF_BEAD_ID}}.

Your first action: bd show {{HANDOFF_BEAD_ID}}

The bead contains the next action, decisions made, dead ends, files
touched, open questions, and session calibrations. Read it completely
before doing anything else. It also tells you how to reach the
previous session if you need to.
```

## Spawn Mechanics

Use the spawn-agent skill's `spawn.sh`:

```
~/.steez/repo/skills/spawn-agent/scripts/spawn.sh split-h \
  --model prometheus \
  --prompt "<spawn prompt>"
```

Parses `TARGET=%N` from output for reporting back to the user. The script handles tmux validation, pane id detection, agent launch, and readiness polling automatically.

## Defaults

- **Skill location:** `skills/handoff/SKILL.md` in the steez repo, symlinked from `~/.claude/skills/handoff/SKILL.md` following the same pattern as other steez skills (spawn-agent, workshop, audit, etc.).
- **Bead title format:** `handoff: <one-line description of in-flight work>`
- **Trigger phrases:** `/handoff` plus natural-language matchers ("hand off this session", "let's hand off", "context is getting big, hand off")
- **Spawn topology:** `split-h` (drops new agent beside the spawning session)

## Acceptance Criteria

- `/handoff` skill exists at `skills/handoff/SKILL.md` in the steez repo
- Symlinked into `~/.claude/skills/handoff` via the same wiring pattern as other steez skills
- Triggering `/handoff` in a real session creates a handoff bead with the full schema filled in from session context
- Pre-flight refuses to proceed with dirty working tree
- Draft is shown for user review/edit before bead creation
- Spawn step creates new tmux pane via `spawn-agent split-h`
- New agent's first action is `bd show <handoff-bead>`
- Old agent stays alive after spawning, idle in its pane
design:
## Meta-decisions

- **Sibling bead model.** The handoff bead is a separate bridge doc pointing back at the in-flight bead. Original stays open. Preserves work-unit integrity, separates durable spec from session-transient state. (Considered: replace original / update in place. Both lose audit fidelity.)

- **Autonomous draft + user review.** Agent fills the schema from session context, then shows the draft for approve/edit/abort. Agent has the context, user has the judgment. (Considered: interactive walkthrough is too slow; fully autonomous has no safety net.)

- **Manual /handoff trigger first.** Hook-based auto-trigger is future work. Ship the minimum viable loop, prove the pattern, then automate.

- **Hard refuse on dirty working tree.** Integrity over convenience. A handoff that inherits half-finished code lies to the new agent, and the new agent has no conversational backstop to catch the lie.

- **Old agent stays alive in its pane.** Last-resort query target only. Does not auto-terminate. User closes when satisfied. The Old Session bead text actively polices against the failure mode where the new agent treats the lifeline as a help desk instead of doing the work itself.

- **Belt-and-suspenders dependency wiring.** Handoff bead `bd dep add`'d as a blocker on the in-flight bead, AND the spawn prompt explicitly names the handoff bead. Free safety.

- **Spawn prompt is minimal (~80 tokens).** Identity assertion + first command. Everything else lives in the bead, where it's consumed once rather than re-shipped on every turn.

- **Old Session bead text uses three-step send/poll/read recipe.** Four-stage evolution: (1) originally drafted as "tmux skill for send-keys, agent-state, agent-history" — load-by-reference; (2) corrected to embedded literal three-step recipe after realizing load-by-reference cost more than embedding (loading tmux skill is ~3500 tokens to get ~150 tokens of recipe); (3) tentatively refined to a hypothetical `agent-send --wait --read` one-liner, assuming the footgun-wrapping script would block and return the response; (4) reverted to the embedded three-step recipe once `agent-send` actually shipped (steez-8lm landed) as pure fire-and-forget — the script wraps the chat-pane send-keys footgun but explicitly leaves response reading to the caller via `agent-state` polling and `agent-history --last`. End state: the three primitives are consolidated under `~/.steez/bin/` but each does one thing, so the bead embeds the three-step recipe. Lesson: spec what exists, not what you wish existed — a load-bearing interface that doesn't exist yet is still a fiction, even if you're planning to build it.

- **Skill lives in steez, not prometheus.** Every dependency is a steez primitive (spawn-agent, agent-send, agent-state, agent-history). Prometheus repo is scoped to the agent definition and its lifecycle infrastructure (hooks, analytics, retros, bin/pro). Cross-agent session utilities belong alongside the other cross-agent skills in `~/.steez/repo/skills/`. The original bead was filed in prometheus because the skill body speaks Prometheus — but the mechanism is agent-agnostic, and co-locating with dependencies is more important than co-locating with the primary consumer. (Superseded pro-58i.)
notes:
## Implementation notes

- The /handoff skill body uses AskUserQuestion for the dirty-tree pre-flight gate (commit / stash / abort) and the draft review gate (approve / edit / abort).
- The autonomous draft step requires the agent to introspect its own session context to fill the schema. This is the hardest UX step to get right — the schema fields need to be specific enough that the agent knows what to capture, but flexible enough to handle the variety of real session shapes.
- Verification is manual: trigger /handoff in a test session at low token count, verify the bead is created correctly, verify the new pane spawns, verify the new agent reads the bead and starts work.
- The spawn prompt template lives in the skill body as a constant. Keep it stable so we can iterate on it cleanly across handoffs.
- Edge case: /handoff with no in-flight bead at all. The flow still works — the handoff bead has no parent to wire as a blocker, but capture/draft/review/create/spawn is unchanged. New agent reads handoff bead and proceeds.

## Dependency status (verified 2026-04-08)

All dependencies are landed and in place on this machine:

- `~/.steez/bin/agent-send` — pure fire-and-forget message delivery, wraps chat-pane send-keys footgun
- `~/.steez/bin/agent-state` — pane state reporter (working / idle / blocked)
- `~/.steez/bin/agent-history` — transcript reader with `--last` flag
- `~/.steez/repo/skills/spawn-agent/scripts/spawn.sh` — pane creation and agent launch, supports `split-h --model prometheus --prompt "..."` with `TARGET=%N` output

Important interface note: `agent-send` does NOT support `--wait --read`. It is pure fire-and-forget by design — response reading is the caller's job via `agent-state` polling and `agent-history --last`. The Old Session schema block in the description embeds the full three-step recipe accordingly.

## Provenance

Originally filed as `pro-58i` in the prometheus beads repo. Relocated here because every dependency lives in steez, and cross-agent session utilities belong alongside other cross-agent skills (spawn-agent, workshop, audit, etc.) rather than in the prometheus agent-definition repo. The prometheus bead was closed with a reason pointing at this one.
close_reason: Skill shipped at skills/handoff/SKILL.md (249dcbc). Symlinked to ~/.claude/skills/handoff. Improvements over original spec: tmux pre-flight guard, stash option dropped (commit-or-abort only), bd create uses --body-file heredoc, workshop-aware path sources from Q&A log and invokes /workshop on resume, workshop bead excluded from dep-add to stay claimable, --dir on spawn for correct cwd.

title: Rename qa and qa-only skills to steez-qa and steez-qa-only
description:

title: refactor: delete tmux skill, scope is now redundant
description:
## Why

After agent operations consolidate into agent-spawn (separate bead), tmux skill has no remaining load-bearing content for this user's workflow. All tmux usage in this environment is agent-pane work, which now lives in agent-spawn. Raw tmux operations (splits, layout discovery, scrollback for non-agent panes, send-keys to shells) are basic enough that a competent agent handles them without a skill.

Keeping a vestigial tmux skill would be dead weight — bytes loaded when accidentally invoked, no value delivered. Delete cleanly.

## Depends On

agent-spawn consolidation bead (must absorb the load-bearing content before tmux skill is removed, otherwise there is a window where the content exists nowhere).

## Deletions

- Remove `~/Projects/Personal/steez/skills/tmux/` directory entirely
- Remove `~/.claude/skills/tmux` symlink (or let the installer reconcile on next run — depends on installer behavior)

## Registry Updates

- `skills.json`: remove the `tmux` entry from the `skills` map. Remove `tmux` from the `infrastructure` category's skills list.
- `~/Projects/Personal/steez/CLAUDE.md`: update the dependency map at the bottom — `agent-state` and `agent-history` are no longer "Used by: tmux skill, agent-spawn skill". Change to: "Used by: agent-spawn skill".

## Doctor Checks

- Run `pro doctor` (in prometheus repo) to verify no checks reference tmux skill. If they do, update or remove.
- Check if steez has a doctor command and run it. Update any tmux-skill checks.
- Search `internal/installer/doctor.go` and `internal/installer/doctor_test.go` for tmux references — remove if present.

## Blast Radius Verification (REQUIRED before deletion)

Before deleting anything, run a full grep to find any remaining references that need to be updated:

```
grep -rn "tmux skill\|skills/tmux\|tmux/SKILL" \
  ~/Projects/Personal/steez \
  ~/.claude/skills \
  ~/.claude/agents \
  ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md \
  ~/Projects/Personal/prometheus
```

Resolve every result before proceeding. If a reference is essential (e.g., a workflow that genuinely needs tmux skill), pause and reconsider the deletion.

## Background

This deletion is the third in a chain: build agent-send → consolidate agent ops into agent-spawn → delete tmux skill. The user's workflow is "agents in panes" — they do not do raw tmux work outside of agent operations. All agent operations now live in agent-spawn after the consolidation bead. Therefore tmux skill has no remaining purpose in this environment.
design:
- The user's actual workflow is "agents in panes" — no raw tmux work outside of agent operations. Therefore tmux skill at any size is dead weight after consolidation.
- Considered shrinking tmux skill to ~50 lines of footgun rules (pane identification, literal \\n issue, capture-pane -p flag). Rejected because the footguns either belong with their corresponding operations (agent-send encapsulates send-keys footguns) or are obvious enough that a competent agent does not need a skill to avoid them.
- Clean deletion is preferred over vestigial pointers. If someone invokes tmux skill by mistake, the descriptions in skills.json and the surrounding agent-spawn skill make it obvious where to go.
acceptance_criteria:
- ~/Projects/Personal/steez/skills/tmux/ deleted
- skills.json no longer has a tmux entry (skills map and infrastructure category)
- CLAUDE.md dependency map updated (agent-state/agent-history "Used by:" no longer mentions tmux skill)
- Doctor checks pass with no tmux references
- Blast radius grep returns zero results for tmux skill references
- Existing agent workflows (spawning, querying, multi-agent overview) still work — they should already be using agent-spawn after the consolidation bead lands
notes:
## Folded in from steez-4nd verification (2026-04-07)

Add to the Registry Updates section above:

- skills.json: also add 'agent-send' to the shared_infra.bin array. The current array is ['agent-history', 'agent-state', 'steez-bd', 'config', 'diff-scope', 'review-log', 'review-read', 'slug'] and is missing 'agent-send'. This was leftover from steez-8lm. The new spawn-agent skill section added in steez-4nd calls ~/.steez/bin/agent-send, so without this fix the installer will not symlink the script and the skill will fail at runtime on a fresh install.

title: refactor: consolidate agent ops into agent-spawn skill
description:
## Why

agent-spawn skill becomes the canonical home for all agent operations: spawn, prompt, read, communicate. Currently agent-pane operations (sending messages, waiting for state, reading responses) live in tmux skill, which means loading ~3500 tokens of tmux content to use ~150 tokens of agent recipes. Moving them to agent-spawn — which a session is far more likely to load when working with agents — eliminates that tradeoff and matches skill scope to user-facing operations.

## Depends On

`agent-send` script bead (steez-8lm). The new skill sections reference `agent-send` directly, so the script must exist before this bead can ship cleanly.

## Files

- `~/Projects/Personal/steez/skills/agent-spawn/SKILL.md` (canonical)
- Edits propagate to `~/.claude/skills/agent-spawn/SKILL.md` via the directory symlink

## Sections to Add

After "Step 3 — Report" in agent-spawn SKILL.md, add a `# Post-Spawn Operations` header followed by these sections:

1. **Sending Input to a Running Agent** — one-liner using `~/.steez/bin/agent-send <pane> "message"`. Note that `agent-send` encapsulates the chat-pane footgun, so callers do not need to know the underlying tmux primitives. Pure fire-and-forget — no waiting, no response reading. If the caller wants to know what the agent did, they read separately on their own clock using the sections below.

2. **Waiting For An Agent To Finish** — explain that `pane_current_command` does NOT work for agent panes. Use `~/.steez/bin/agent-state <pane>` instead. Document the state enum: working, idle, blocked:question, blocked:permission, blocked:unknown. Provide the polling loop pattern as the canonical (and only) approach: send the message, then loop on `agent-state <pane>` with `sleep 3` until state is no longer `working`. Note that this blocks the parent agent — use it sparingly and prefer fire-and-forget when possible.

3. **Reading Agent Output** — `agent-history --last` for the last prompt/response pair, `--blocked` for the pending tool call, and `--history N` for recent pairs. agent-history is the canonical reader because it parses the structured JSONL transcript and gives you turn-aware data.

   **Fallback:** when `agent-history` cannot resolve the transcript (no `@session_id` pane variable, transcript file rotated or missing, pane not recognized as a known agent), fall back to `tmux capture-pane -t <pane> -p -S -` for raw scrollback. The structured reader is preferred but the raw capture always works. Document both so callers know which to reach for.

4. **Multi-Agent Overview** — `agent-state --all`, `--detail`, `--read`, `--layout`. Brief reference for scanning all panes.

## Frontmatter Update

Expand the description field to claim the broader scope. Add new trigger phrases including: "send the agent a message", "ask the other agent", "query the previous session", "check what that agent is doing", "read the agent's response", "wait for the agent to finish". Preserve the existing spawn/launch/orchestrate triggers.

## skills.json Update

Update the agent-spawn entry description to reflect the broader scope. Suggested text (max 80 chars per CLAUDE.md convention): \"Spawn, prompt, read from, and communicate with AI agents across tmux panes\" (75 chars).

## Notes

- The sections should reference `agent-send`, `agent-state`, and `agent-history` by name rather than embedding raw tmux recipes. The scripts encapsulate the recipes; the skill teaches when to use the scripts.
- Keep the new content tight. Principle: load-bearing knowledge that would be a footgun without the script lives in the script. The skill teaches WHEN and WHY, not the recipe itself.
- This bead is part of a larger consolidation: agent-send (prerequisite, steez-8lm) → this bead → tmux skill deletion (downstream, steez-d3b).
- Design note: there is no synchronous query primitive. The earlier spec included a "Querying Another Agent" section bundling send+wait+read; that was removed because the actual usage model is async/email — sender fires and walks away, reader checks the response when convenient. The polling loop in section 2 exists for the rare case where a caller genuinely must block, with a warning that it's a sharp tool.
design:
- Skill cohesion principle: skills map to operations, not to underlying tools. If you are talking to an agent in another pane, that is agent work — even if the underlying primitive is tmux send-keys.
- Token economy: load-by-reference is only cheaper than embed if (referenced size + load cost) > recipe size. Loading the tmux skill cost ~3500 tokens to get ~150 tokens of agent content. Consolidating eliminates that trade.
- The current carve-out in the tmux skill ("EXCEPTION: do not use this for spawning agents — use agent-spawn") is a smell. The boundary is already telling you it is in the wrong place.
- No synchronous query primitive. The earlier spec proposed a "Querying Another Agent" section bundling send + wait + read; it was removed because the actual usage model is async/email — sender fires and walks away, reader checks the response when convenient. The polling loop in section 2 exists for the rare case where a caller genuinely must block, with an explicit warning that it is a sharp tool.
- agent-history is the canonical reader for agent panes, but `tmux capture-pane -p -S -` is the documented fallback when the structured reader cannot resolve the transcript. Both paths are taught.

- Section 2 documents the `agent-state` polling loop as the canonical (and only) wait pattern, with a warning that it blocks the parent
- Section 3 prefers `agent-history --last` / `--blocked` / `--history N`, AND documents `tmux capture-pane -t <pane> -p -S -` as the fallback when the transcript is unresolvable
- Section 4 references `agent-state --all`, `--detail`, `--read`, `--layout` for multi-agent overview
- No raw `tmux send-keys` recipes embedded in the new sections — agent-send is the canonical interface
- Frontmatter description expanded with new trigger phrases (send the agent a message, ask the other agent, check what that agent is doing, read the agent's response, wait for the agent to finish), preserving existing spawn/launch/orchestrate triggers
- skills.json description updated to "Spawn, prompt, read from, and communicate with AI agents across tmux panes" (75 chars, max 80 per CLAUDE.md)
- Skill is invokable for all agent operations, not just spawning

Expanded frontmatter description with the seven new trigger phrases. Updated skills.json description to 'Spawn, prompt, read from, and communicate with AI agents across tmux panes' (74 chars). No raw tmux send-keys recipes embedded. Committed in f458f9f. Unblocks steez-d3b (tmux skill deletion).

title: feat: build agent-send script for chat-pane message delivery
description:
## Why

Wrap the chat-pane delayed-Enter footgun in a single script so callers never need to know the recipe. Sending text to a Claude Code or Codex pane via raw `tmux send-keys -t %X "msg" Enter` silently fails because the composer needs Enter as a separate keystroke after a brief delay — the obvious incantation leaves the message sitting in the composer unsubmitted. Encoding the working pattern once eliminates the trap.

## Scope

Pure fire-and-forget sender. No flags beyond `--help`. The script sends a message and exits. Reading responses is the caller's job using the existing `agent-state` and `agent-history` primitives, on their own clock.

We explicitly chose NOT to bundle send + wait + read into one tool. The actual usage model between agents is email (async, polling-based, reader checks when convenient), not RPC. Bundling wait/read flags would push the wrong default and bake an unnecessary primitive into the sender.

## Where

`~/.steez/repo/shared/steez/bin/agent-send` (canonical location). Symlinked from `~/.steez/bin/agent-send` via the existing installer mechanism.

## Signature

```
agent-send <pane> "message"
agent-send -h | --help
```

- Pane id can be `%N` (stable, preferred) or `session:window.pane` — match what `agent-state` and `agent-history` already accept.
- Multiline messages: use real newlines inside a quoted argument (`"line one\nline two"` does NOT work — `\n` is sent as literal backslash + n). Heredoc-quoting works: `"$(cat <<'EOF' ... EOF)"`.

## Implementation Notes

- Bash, ~25-40 lines including help and arg parsing.
- **Pane validation:** call `~/.steez/bin/agent-state <pane>` before sending. If the pane is not a recognized agent (claude or codex), exit non-zero with a clear stderr message. This catches the wrong-target footgun.
- **Escape-safe send via tmux buffer** (NOT shell-escaped send-keys with the message as a string argument):
  ```
  printf '%s' "\$MSG" | tmux load-buffer -b agent-send-\$\$ -
  tmux paste-buffer -b agent-send-\$\$ -t "\$PANE" -d
  ```
  load-buffer reads bytes from stdin verbatim, so backticks, dollar signs, and quotes in the message body cannot be mangled. The `-d` flag deletes the buffer after pasting.
- **Delayed Enter** to actually submit the composer:
  ```
  sleep 0.3
  tmux send-keys -t "\$PANE" Enter
  ```
  (Or the equivalent `\; run-shell -d 0.3 '...'` form. Both work; pick whichever is cleaner.)
- **Composer-clear retry:** after the delayed Enter, briefly capture the bottom of the pane. If the message is still visible in the composer area (i.e., not submitted), send Enter once more and re-check. Document the retry as belt-and-suspenders.
- Exit codes: 0 (sent), 1 (generic error: bad args, send failed), 2 (pane not a recognized agent).

## Installer Plumbing

agent-send must be added to ALL five locations that currently list `agent-state` and `agent-history`. Missing any one will cause doctor checks or tests to fail. Locations:

1. `cmd/steez/install.go` — `binSymlinks` slice (around lines 122-132)
2. `internal/installer/doctor.go` — `expected` slice in `checkBinSymlinks` (around lines 137-141)
3. `internal/installer/doctor_test.go` — test fixture (around lines 39-40, 60, 195)
4. `internal/installer/integration_test.go` — test fixtures (around lines 69-70, 169-170)
5. `CLAUDE.md` — helper scripts list/table near the top, plus the dependency block at the bottom

## Acceptance Criteria

- Script exists at `~/.steez/repo/shared/steez/bin/agent-send`
- Symlinked from `~/.steez/bin/agent-send` after `make install` / `steez install`
- `agent-send <pane> "msg"` submits the message to the agent's composer and exits 0
- `agent-send <pane> "msg with \$vars and backticks"` sends literal characters with no shell-expansion mangling
- Multiline messages (real newlines in the quoted argument) submit correctly
- Pane validation rejects non-agent panes with exit 2 and a clear stderr message
- Composer-clear retry resends Enter if the first attempt did not submit
- All five installer plumbing locations updated
- Verified end-to-end against a real Claude Code pane (send a message, confirm it landed in the transcript via `agent-history --last`)

## Background

The original spec proposed `--wait`, `--read`, `--stdin` flags and framed this as the third member of an "inter-agent comms primitive set." A design discussion stripped that down: synchronous RPC between agents is rarely the right pattern (the parent shouldn't block on the child), the actual model is email-style async, and the receive side is already covered by `agent-state` and `agent-history --last`. agent-send is therefore pure send. If a caller genuinely needs to block until a response lands, they can write a five-line shell loop using the existing primitives — we don't need to ship that as a flag.
design:
- Script wraps the chat-pane delayed-Enter footgun in one place so the recipe lives ONCE in the codebase, not in every skill body that needs to send a message.
- Pure fire-and-forget. Send the message, exit. No flags beyond --help. The script is a sender, not an RPC client.
- Email model, not RPC: senders fire and walk away; readers check `agent-state` / `agent-history --last` on their own clock when they want to know what happened. Synchronous query is rarely the right pattern between agents because blocking the parent on the child defeats most of the point of having two agents.
- Symmetric naming with `agent-state` and `agent-history`. Three primitives, distinct responsibilities: send, read state, read transcript. None of them blocks; none of them knows about the others' data.
- Escape safety via tmux `load-buffer` + `paste-buffer` instead of `send-keys` with a quoted string argument. The buffer path passes bytes verbatim, so messages containing dollar signs, backticks, or quotes do not get mangled by shell or tmux parsing.
- Pane validation upfront via `agent-state` to catch the wrong-target footgun (sending into a vim pane, a shell, or a pane running an unrelated TUI).
acceptance_criteria:
- Script exists at ~/.steez/repo/shared/steez/bin/agent-send
- Symlinked from ~/.steez/bin/agent-send after `make install` / `steez install`
- `agent-send <pane> "msg"` submits the message to the agent's composer and exits 0
- `agent-send <pane> 'msg with $vars and `backticks`'` sends literal characters with no shell-expansion mangling (escape safety verified)
- Multiline messages (real newlines in a quoted argument or heredoc) submit correctly
- Pane validation rejects non-agent panes with exit 2 and a clear stderr message
- Composer-clear retry resends Enter once if the first delayed Enter did not submit the composer
- All five installer plumbing locations updated (cmd/steez/install.go, internal/installer/doctor.go, internal/installer/doctor_test.go, internal/installer/integration_test.go, CLAUDE.md)
- Verified end-to-end against a real Claude Code pane: send a message, confirm via `agent-history --last` that the message landed in the transcript
- The chat-pane delayed-Enter pattern is fully encapsulated — no caller ever needs to know it
close_reason: Built shared/steez/bin/agent-send wrapping the chat-pane delayed-Enter footgun. Script uses tmux load-buffer/paste-buffer for escape-safe verbatim sends, separates Enter as a delayed keystroke, validates pane via agent-state (exit 2 on non-agent), traps for buffer cleanup, and retries Enter if state stays idle. Plumbed into all six installer locations (cmd/steez/install.go, internal/tui/setup.go, internal/installer/doctor.go, doctor_test.go, integration_test.go, CLAUDE.md). Verified end-to-end against a fresh Claude Code pane: plain message, message with $vars/backticks/quotes, multiline with real newlines — all landed in the transcript exactly as sent per agent-history --last. Negative tests: non-agent pane → exit 2, missing args → exit 1. go build + go test ./... clean, steez doctor passes (39/0/1, warning unrelated). Independent verification flagged a missed sixth location (internal/tui/setup.go binSymlinks slice — the bead spec listed five but the README-documented setup path has its own parallel slice); fixed in the same session and memory entry written about grep-verifying spec enumerations going forward.

title: Inline self-report in 8 diagnostic skills
description:
## Context

Implementation slice split off from steez-31x (preamble audit). The four self-report design decisions are locked in steez-31x notes under the "2026-04-07 PM — Self-report: salvaged, not deleted" section. This bead is implementation only — read that section first for the template and rationale.

## What to do

Add a "## Self-Report" final-step instruction to each of these 8 SKILL.md files:

1. skills/investigate/SKILL.md
2. skills/qa/SKILL.md
3. skills/audit/SKILL.md
4. skills/cso/SKILL.md
5. skills/sharpen-skill/SKILL.md
6. skills/design-review/SKILL.md
7. skills/workshop/SKILL.md
8. skills/office-hours/SKILL.md

The instruction template (from steez-31x notes — copied here for cold-start convenience):

> At the end of every skill run, include a "## Self-Report" section in the final user-facing message with:
> - **Rating**: N/10 against this skill's specific quality criteria (define per skill)
> - **What went well**: one line
> - **What didn't**: one line (if nothing, say "nothing notable")
> - **What would make this a 10**: one concrete, actionable suggestion
> - **STEEZ FINDING** (only if rating below 8 AND the issue is a steez tooling problem, NOT a user app bug, network error, or auth failure on user's site): one-line description of the underlying issue, prefixed literally with "STEEZ FINDING:" so the retro consumer can grep for it.

## Per-skill quality criteria

Each skill needs its own quality criteria for the rating. Don't reuse a generic 0-10 scale — the rating is more honest when it's grounded in what THIS skill is supposed to do well. Suggested criteria (refine on implementation):

- **investigate**: did we hit root cause vs symptom? did the fix actually verify? did we explore enough alternatives before committing?
- **qa**: coverage of critical paths, severity calibration of bugs found, false-positive rate
- **audit**: depth of analysis, did we miss any high-severity issues, evidence quality
- **cso**: did we surface real security issues vs theoretical, blast-radius accuracy
- **sharpen-skill**: did the sharpened version actually improve the skill, or just rearrange it
- **design-review**: did we catch real visual/UX issues vs nitpicks, did fixes actually land
- **workshop**: did we apply the lenses honestly or rubber-stamp the user's hunch, did the disposition (bead/kill/memory/graduate) match reality
- **office-hours**: did we expose real demand reality vs build a flattering brief

## Preamble interaction

The self-report block currently exists at preamble/sections/self-report.md and is injected via tiers 2-4 in preamble/tiers.json into 19 skills. The preamble system is being dismantled separately under steez-31x. Two valid approaches:

1. **Inline directly in each target SKILL.md body** (recommended): write the new self-report instruction directly into each of the 8 skills' SKILL.md as a final-step section. Skip the preamble entirely. This makes the 8 skills preamble-clean for self-report regardless of when the broader preamble dismantling lands.

2. **Edit preamble/sections/self-report.md and limit injection**: rewrite the existing preamble section to match the new template, edit preamble/tiers.json so only the 8 target skills inject it. Less file churn but couples this work to the dying preamble system.

Go with option 1 — the preamble is dying, and inlining is forward-compatible.

## Done criteria

- All 8 SKILL.md files have a Self-Report final-step section using the template above
- Each skill has its own quality criteria filled in (not generic 0-10)
- A live test on at least one skill: invoke it, verify the final message includes the Self-Report block as instructed
- Commit on main with conventional commit message
- Independent verification via prometheus-verification subagent (3+ file edits = non-trivial, verification mandatory per Prometheus definition)

## Downstream consumer

steez-z9l (Extend retro to create skill-finding beads from low-rated self-reports) is blocked on this bead. Once this lands, that bead becomes ready and the retro side can be implemented.

## Reference

- steez-31x notes (2026-04-07 PM section) — full design rationale and decision history
- Current preamble self-report block: preamble/sections/self-report.md
- Current preamble tier mapping: preamble/tiers.json

The error_json bash function on line 28 is a separate concern — leave it or fix it independently. title: Build skillify skill from spec
description:
Build the skillify skill at ~/Projects/Personal/steez/skills/skillify/SKILL.md from the spec at ~/Projects/Personal/steez/skillify-skill-spec.md.

## What skillify does
A meta-skill that captures a session's repeatable process as a reusable SKILL.md file. The user finishes some workflow in their current session and invokes /skillify there — same session, with the workflow's inputs and outputs still in the conversation. Skillify analyzes that conversation, interviews the user, then spawns a Task subagent to synthesize the new SKILL.md in a fresh context window.

## Architecture: inline analysis + interview, fresh-context synthesis via Task subagent
Skillify runs inline. No `context: fork`. It is invoked in the same session that produced the workflow being captured — the conversation, tool calls, and corrections in the active context window are its primary source material. agent-history is only a fallback for the case where the session has been compacted and the in-window conversation is incomplete.

The load-bearing design choice is what happens AFTER analysis and interview: skillify does NOT write the SKILL.md directly from the parent session's context. Instead it spawns a Task subagent and hands it the structured spec assembled from the interview. The subagent runs in a fresh context window — it has no access to the original workflow conversation, no corrections, no false starts, no exploratory tool calls. All it sees is the spec.

Why this matters:
- **Cold-start completeness is enforced by construction.** If the subagent (zero session noise, only the spec as input) can produce a working SKILL.md, then the spec is provably self-contained. You cannot accidentally author a skill that secretly depends on context from the session that created it, because the writer has no access to that context.
- **The synthesis is clean.** The parent session is full of meandering conversation, corrections, dead ends. A fresh context produces a more coherent skill body than an inline writer biased by all that noise.
- **Skillify itself stays lightweight in the parent session.** Analysis + interview happen inline (where they need conversation context and AskUserQuestion). Writing happens in the subagent (where it doesn't need any of that).
- **Validation still happens inline.** After the subagent returns, skillify reads the file back in the parent session, verifies YAML parses, updates skills.json if applicable, and reports the path to the user.

## Implementation
1. Create directory: skills/skillify/
2. Create skills/skillify/SKILL.md with:
   - Frontmatter: two fields only — `name: skillify` and `description` with pushy multi-line trigger phrases ('skillify', 'capture this as a skill', 'turn this into a skill', 'bottle this workflow', etc.). No `context: fork` (skillify is inline). No `allowed-tools` (omit to inherit the default broad toolset, matching official skill-creator). The Task tool is part of the default toolset, so the inline skill can spawn the writer subagent without any special permission.
   - Body sections in this order:
     - Intro: explain the skill is invoked in the same session that just produced the workflow, and that the in-window conversation is the primary source
     - **Step 1: Analyze the session** — read the in-window conversation. Use agent-history ONLY as a fallback if the session has been compacted and parts of the workflow are missing. Identify the workflow, the inputs/parameters, the distinct steps in order, the success artifacts, where the user corrected or steered, what tools were essential vs incidental
     - **Step 2: Interview the user** — port the spec's 4 rounds of AskUserQuestion (high-level confirmation, more details, breaking down each step, final questions). All questions go through AskUserQuestion, never plain text
     - **Step 3: Synthesize via Task subagent** — assemble the structured spec from the interview output (name, description, allowed-tools list if needed, body steps with success criteria, save location, cold-start-complete enough that a fresh context can write it). Spawn a Task subagent with the spec as the entire prompt and instructions to write the SKILL.md at the chosen path. The subagent's prompt MUST be self-contained — no "based on the conversation above," because the subagent has no conversation above
     - **Step 4: Validate and report** — read the written file back in the parent session, check YAML frontmatter parses, update skills.json if the save location is steez-managed, tell the user where the skill was saved and how to invoke it
3. Add skillify entry to skills.json under the `meta` category (alongside skill-creator/autoplan/sharpen-skill). Description max 80 chars.
4. Verify by reading the written SKILL.md back and checking frontmatter parses.

## Key design decisions already settled (do NOT re-litigate)
- INLINE skill, NOT forked. Runs in the same session that has the workflow. The in-window conversation is the primary source material
- Synthesis step delegates to a Task subagent for cold-start cleanness. The subagent's prompt is the structured spec from the interview, and only that. No "see the conversation above" references
- agent-history is a FALLBACK only — used if the session has been compacted and parts of the workflow are missing from the in-window context. Not the primary source
- NO `allowed-tools` field — omit to inherit the default toolset. Matches official skill-creator pattern. The Task tool is part of that default, so the subagent spawn works
- NO template variables like {{sessionMemory}} or {{userMessages}} — the inline part has the conversation in context, and the subagent has the spec
- NO `when_to_use` or `arguments` frontmatter fields — those don't exist in Claude Code. Use description for trigger logic and $ARGUMENTS / $1 in the body
- NO preamble block — preamble system is being dismantled (see steez-31x). Workshop is the most recent reference for the no-preamble style
- The agent-history fallback call should NOT include the `| jq '.'` pipe — trust steez-e5b will land
- Save location options: steez-managed (default, skills/ in repo), repo-local (.claude/skills/), or personal (~/.claude/skills/)
- Composition with skill-creator: skillify captures raw workflow from a session, skill-creator refines and benchmarks an existing skill. Do NOT bake eval infrastructure into skillify

## Reference material
- Spec: skillify-skill-spec.md (repo root)
- Example steez skills for frontmatter format: skills/workshop/SKILL.md (most recent, no preamble), skills/agent-spawn/SKILL.md, skills/investigate/SKILL.md, skills/codex/SKILL.md
- skills.json for category conventions (skillify goes under `meta`)
- Existing skill-creator at ~/.claude/plugins/marketplaces/claude-plugins-official/plugins/skill-creator/skills/skill-creator/SKILL.md — read its description field for pushy-trigger inspiration AND note it ships with only name + description in frontmatter
- Claude Code skills docs: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/skills

## Definition of done
- skills/skillify/SKILL.md exists with valid frontmatter (two fields: name, description)
- The body has 4 steps: analyze (inline), interview (inline AskUserQuestion), synthesize (Task subagent with self-contained spec), validate (inline read-back + skills.json update + user report)
- skills.json has a skillify entry under the meta category
- Reading the file back confirms YAML parses
- A commit on main with the new files (skillify only — leave the unrelated workshop working-tree changes alone)
close_reason: Skill built and committed. skills/skillify/SKILL.md (166 lines, 2-field frontmatter, 4-step body: analyze session inline, interview via AskUserQuestion, synthesize via Task subagent, validate and report). skills.json updated under meta category. YAML validates. Commit 97301e5 on main.

title: Audit and slim down skill preamble system
description:
The managed preamble (75 lines, synced to 22+ skills) was ported from gstack and may contain dead or redundant sections now that prometheus agent definition and hooks handle the same concerns. Needs investigation before removal.

Sections to evaluate:
- Session tracking (PID files): appears dead, hooks handle session tracking via tmux pane vars. Verify nothing reads ~/.steez/sessions/.
- Branch detection: model can run git branch on demand. Check if any skill logic actually uses _BRANCH.
- Repo mode: hardcoded solo. Check if anything checks REPO_MODE.
- Writing rules: duplicated by prometheus definition. Check if skills are ever invoked outside prometheus.
- Completion status protocol: check if any downstream tooling parses DONE/BLOCKED/etc.

title: Implement steez-agent-history script
description:
New script at shared/steez/bin/steez-agent-history. Bash wrapper + embedded Python3 parser that reads JSONL transcripts from Claude Code and Codex CLI sessions and returns structured conversation JSON.

Plan: ~/.claude/plans/federated-greeting-flame.md

## Input resolution

Three input modes:
1. Pane ID (e.g., %42): shells out to `steez-agent-state %42 --detail`, extracts `detail.transcript_path` and top-level `agent` from JSON response.
2. Direct path (e.g., /path/to/file.jsonl): agent type inferred from path (~/.claude/ = claude, ~/.codex/ = codex). Error if unrecognizable, with --agent claude|codex override.
3. No target: uses $TMUX_PANE env var if set, otherwise errors.

## Three modes

--last: Returns last human prompt + last assistant text response.
--blocked: Returns the pending tool_use awaiting approval/answer.
--history N: Returns last N human/assistant pairs in chronological order.

## Output schemas

--last: {"agent": "claude|codex", "prompt": "...", "response": "..."}
--blocked: {"agent": "claude|codex", "tool": "Bash|...", "input": {...}, "question": "... (AskUserQuestion only)"}
--history N: {"agent": "claude|codex", "pairs": [{"prompt": "...", "response": "..."}, ...]}
Errors: {"error": "description"}

## Claude JSONL parsing rules

Human prompts: type=="user" AND message.content is string AND isMeta is falsy AND isSidechain is falsy.
Assistant responses: type=="assistant" AND isSidechain is falsy. CRITICAL: content blocks are spread across multiple JSONL lines sharing the same message.id (delta pattern). Group by message.id, collect all content blocks, extract only type:"text" blocks. Ignore thinking and tool_use blocks.
Tool results: type=="user" AND message.content is list. Skip — not conversation.

## Codex JSONL parsing rules

Human prompts: type=="event_msg" AND payload.type=="user_message". Text in payload.message. Ignore response_item with role:"user" — that's system-injected context.
Assistant responses (--last): use type=="event_msg" AND payload.type=="task_complete" — last_agent_message field has final text.
Assistant responses (--history): collect type=="response_item" AND payload.type=="message" AND payload.role=="assistant", extract output_text content blocks.

## --blocked mode

Claude: last type:"assistant" message with a tool_use content block that has no corresponding tool_result in a subsequent type:"user" line. Returns tool name + key input fields. For AskUserQuestion specifically, extracts input.questions[0].question (the input is {questions: [{question: "...", header: "...", options: [...]}]}).
Codex: last response_item with type:"function_call" that has no matching function_call_output by call_id.

## Performance

tail -500 + python3. If 500 lines has no human prompt, fallback to tail -2000. Target <200ms for --last.

## Verification

1. Run against a known Claude transcript path with --last, --history 3, verify output matches conversation.
2. Run against a known Codex transcript path with --last, --history 3.
3. Run --blocked against transcript where last tool_use has no tool_result.
4. Run with nonexistent path — verify clean error JSON.
5. Performance: time steez-agent-history /path/to/large.jsonl --last — confirm <200ms.

Done when: steez-agent-history <transcript_path> --last returns correct last prompt and response for both Claude and Codex transcripts.
close_reason: Script implemented and verified against live Claude and Codex transcripts. All modes (--last, --blocked, --history N) working. Error handling covers nonexistent files, unrecognizable paths, missing modes. Performance: 80-114ms on transcripts up to 2276 lines.

title: Fix Codex working state detection
description:
The current Codex idle detection in steez-agent-state checks for the › (U+203A) prompt character in the last 10 lines of pane content. This is wrong — the › character is visible in both idle and active states. When Codex is actively working, the previous prompt text (prefixed with ›) remains visible in the pane while new output streams above it.

Need to find a reliable signal that distinguishes Codex working from idle. Possible approaches:
- Check if the last line is the status bar vs active output
- Look for Codex's working indicators (• bullets, streaming text)
- Check pane_current_command or process activity
- Poll the Codex SQLite state DB for thread status

Current code: bin/agent-state lines 113-120 (detect_state function, codex branch).

title: refactor: restructure repo — move project docs to root, shared runtime to shared/steez/
description:
Restructure repo to separate project docs from shared runtime infra. Currently skills/steez/ conflates both concerns.

## What changes

1. **Move project docs to repo root:** ETHOS.md, ARCHITECTURE.md, FORK_MANIFEST.md move to repo root. Merge skills/steez/CLAUDE.md (222 lines, rich) into root CLAUDE.md. Merge skills/steez/README.md (159 lines) into root README.md.

2. **Move shared runtime to shared/steez/:** bin/ (6 bash helper scripts) moves from skills/steez/bin/ to shared/steez/bin/. Browse build artifacts (src/, dist/, scripts/, bin/, bun.lock, package.json) move from skills/browse/ to shared/steez/browse/. skills/steez/package.json moves to shared/steez/package.json.

3. **Delete skills/steez/ entirely.** skills/browse/ retains only SKILL.md.

4. **New path resolution via installer-managed symlinks (Option C):**
   - Install creates: ~/.steez/repo -> <user's checkout>
   - Install creates: ~/.steez/bin/steez-config -> ~/.steez/repo/shared/steez/bin/steez-config (and 5 more bin scripts)
   - Install creates: ~/.steez/bin/browse -> ~/.steez/repo/shared/steez/browse/dist/browse
   - Executables: hardcoded ~/.steez/bin/X (no variables, no ${:-}, matches gstack)
   - Documents: hardcoded ~/.steez/repo/ETHOS.md (through repo symlink)
   - Runtime state: ${STEEZ_HOME:-$HOME/.steez} (override for testing, matches gstack's ${GSTACK_HOME:-...})
   - No STEEZ_REPO env var. No STEEZ_BIN alias. No marker-based discovery.
   - Symlinks chain through ~/.steez/repo — re-pointing one symlink cascades everywhere.

5. **Update preamble templates** (preamble-bash.md, beads-context.md, search.md):
   - Remove STEEZ_BIN variable entirely
   - Change STEEZ_HOME to ${STEEZ_HOME:-$HOME/.steez}
   - Update bin calls to ~/.steez/bin/X
   - Update ETHOS.md ref to ~/.steez/repo/ETHOS.md
   - Run steez sync to propagate to all 33 SKILL.md managed preamble blocks.

6. **Bulk replace ~180 call sites** in skill bodies outside managed preamble:
   - $STEEZ_BIN/steez-X and ~/.claude/skills/steez/bin/steez-X -> ~/.steez/bin/steez-X
   - Browse binary fallback chains -> ~/.steez/bin/browse
   - ETHOS.md refs -> ~/.steez/repo/ETHOS.md
   - Cross-skill refs (autoplan reading other SKILL.md) stay as ~/.claude/skills/steez-X/ (existing skill symlinks)

7. **Update Go code:**
   - install.go: create ~/.steez/repo symlink + ~/.steez/bin/ directory with 7 symlinks
   - doctor.go: validate ~/.steez/repo + ~/.steez/bin/ symlinks
   - browse.go: update browse directory path to shared/steez/browse/
   - setup.go: update shared home and browse paths

8. **Clean up browse TypeScript:** delete dead code (find-browse.ts, bin/find-browse, find-browse.test.ts), update sidebar-agent.ts, server.ts, test fixtures. Drop .codex/.agents/.claude marker discovery.

9. **Add old-path guardrail** to steez sync --check (grep for ~/.claude/skills/steez/ in skill files).

10. **Delete dead vendor check** in design-html/SKILL.md (lines 274-275, vendor file never existed).

## Key design decisions (see --design field for full rationale + Codex/Claude session IDs)
- Hardcoded executable paths via installer-managed symlinks (matches gstack's Claude host pattern)
- ${STEEZ_HOME:-default} for runtime state override (matches gstack's ${GSTACK_HOME:-...})
- No STEEZ_REPO env var — re-pointing ~/.steez/repo symlink serves same purpose, works for Go/bash/TS uniformly
- Symlinks chain: ~/.steez/bin/X -> ~/.steez/repo/shared/steez/bin/X -> <checkout>/shared/steez/bin/X
- steez sync scope stays narrow (preamble only). Bulk replace for bodies. steez-e57 handles long-term.

## Blocks
- steez-e57 (sync compiler) depends on this landing first
design:
## Design Decisions (confirmed via Codex consult + Claude review)

### Path resolution: ~/.steez/ as stable root with installer-managed symlinks (Option C)

Three categories of paths, each with a clear pattern:

**Executables (bin scripts, browse binary):**
- Installer creates: ~/.steez/bin/steez-config -> ~/.steez/repo/shared/steez/bin/steez-config
- Installer creates: ~/.steez/bin/browse -> ~/.steez/repo/shared/steez/browse/dist/browse
- All SKILL.md call sites use: ~/.steez/bin/steez-config, ~/.steez/bin/browse
- Hardcoded paths, no variables, no ${:-} — matches gstack's approach for Claude host
- Symlinks chain through ~/.steez/repo so re-pointing the repo symlink cascades everywhere

**Runtime state:**
- Preamble sets: STEEZ_HOME="${STEEZ_HOME:-$HOME/.steez}"
- ${:-} override matches gstack's ${GSTACK_HOME:-$HOME/.gstack} pattern
- Allows testing against clean state: STEEZ_HOME=/tmp/test steez-config get proactive

**Documents (ETHOS.md etc.):**
- ETHOS.md moves to repo root, referenced as: ~/.steez/repo/ETHOS.md
- Hardcoded path through repo symlink, no variables — matches gstack's approach

### Why Option C over Option B (inline ${STEEZ_REPO:-...})
- gstack hardcodes executable paths for Claude host — steez should too
- ${STEEZ_REPO:-$HOME/.steez/repo} was steez's own extrapolation, not from gstack
- A stable filesystem path is more self-contained than an inline variable expansion
- 25 chars (~/.steez/bin/steez-config) vs 60+ chars (${STEEZ_REPO:-...}/shared/steez/bin/steez-config)
- Simpler for steez-e57 compiler to generate
- The repo already tried a runtime resolver (find-browse) — it died as dead code. Stable installed paths won.

### Why no STEEZ_REPO env var
- Variables do not persist between bash blocks (separate shells)
- Re-pointing ~/.steez/repo symlink achieves the same override for CI/worktrees
- Symlink override works for ALL consumers (Go CLI, bash, TypeScript) uniformly
- STEEZ_REPO was designed for flexibility that even gstack (supporting 3 hosts) did not need

### Installer symlink structure
~/.steez/
  repo -> <user's checkout>              # installer creates
  bin/
    steez-config -> ~/.steez/repo/shared/steez/bin/steez-config
    steez-slug -> ~/.steez/repo/shared/steez/bin/steez-slug
    steez-diff-scope -> ~/.steez/repo/shared/steez/bin/steez-diff-scope
    steez-review-log -> ~/.steez/repo/shared/steez/bin/steez-review-log
    steez-review-read -> ~/.steez/repo/shared/steez/bin/steez-review-read
    steez-bd -> ~/.steez/repo/shared/steez/bin/steez-bd
    browse -> ~/.steez/repo/shared/steez/browse/dist/browse

### Self-contained bash blocks
- Each bash code block runs in a SEPARATE shell. Variables do not persist.
- ~/.steez/bin/X requires zero variable setup — maximally self-contained
- No dependency on AI propagating variables across blocks
- $B (browse binary alias) still set per-block as before — LLM carries the value

### Skill body path migration strategy
- One-time bulk find-and-replace for all call sites across 20+ SKILL.md files
- Old patterns: $STEEZ_BIN/steez-config, ~/.claude/skills/steez/bin/X, browse fallback chains
- New pattern: ~/.steez/bin/X (executables), ~/.steez/repo/X (documents)
- steez sync handles managed preamble (inside markers) automatically
- Add old-path detection to steez sync --check as a guardrail
- Long-term: steez-e57 compiler takes over skill body maintenance

### Project-level installs: visibility-local, runtime-global
- Project-level skill installs control WHERE Claude sees the skill
- The shared runtime (bin scripts, browse binary) is always global via ~/.steez/bin/
- No version isolation per project — one steez checkout serves all skills everywhere

### Codex session IDs
- Initial consult (repo symlink decision): 019d456c-c595-7842-b660-f060654cda19
- STEEZ_BIN challenge (drop alias): 019d4578-eee4-7010-82ca-63079f0cbe83
- Adversarial review (project-level + env override): 019d4594-d1a2-7823-8eb5-11c08e8985c0
- Variable vs hardcode in bodies: 019d45a7-7331-74b0-9054-ec8202ab5858
- How to propagate paths: 019d45ab-8a6e-7952-9789-75950dc0dcbc
- Option C validation (browse move + symlinks): 019d4605-184d-7d11-967a-9d213675034c
- Option C adversarial challenge (inline vs symlinks): 019d4608-ad6b-7033-9376-0ef2df1e871d

title: QA: steez TUI install flow + CLI commands
description:
Run /steez-qa against the steez TUI and CLI to find and fix bugs.

## Test surface

### TUI (steez setup)
- Profile selection (starter/all/custom) renders correctly
- Custom picker: viewport scrolls properly in small terminals and tmux
- Migration gate: detects stow fold, shows correct commands, loops until resolved
- Preflight: shows skill count, browse binary warning when applicable
- Install step: creates symlinks, shows per-skill status, summary states (success/partial/no-op)
- Returning user skips splash
- q/esc quits cleanly at every step
- NO_COLOR disables all styling

### CLI commands
- steez install starter/all/<skill names> with --dry-run, --force, --browse
- steez uninstall <skills> and --all
- steez list shows installed skills with valid/broken status
- steez info <skill> with fuzzy matching (typos suggest correct name)
- steez doctor and --fix mode
- steez update on clean/dirty/diverged repos
- steez version shows version, go, os, repo path
- Unknown commands show helpful error

### Edge cases
- Browse binary not built: warning shown, not error
- Already-installed skills: idempotent (no error)
- Empty registry: list shows helpful message
- Small terminal: picker viewport scrolls, doesn't overflow
- Permissions: symlink creation fails gracefully with clear message

## Not in scope
- Browse binary build (separate feature)
- TUI visual design polish (separate bead)

title: Move netsuite-browse skill to steez repo + narrow install path
description:
## Context
The browse binary (steez/skills/browse/) has a NetSuite protocol layer (ns commands) built in. The companion netsuite-browse SKILL.md teaches Claude how to use these commands. Currently the skill lives in the private NetSuite monorepo but the binary lives in the public steez repo. They're one logical unit split across two repos.

## Codex recommendation (2026-03-31)
Treat netsuite-browse as part of the public API surface of browse. Publish them together from steez. Let the private monorepo consume that published artifact instead of owning a second copy.

## Scope

### 1. Move skill to steez
- Copy netsuite-browse SKILL.md from monorepo to steez/skills/netsuite-browse/
- Add to skills.json under infrastructure category with requires: browse-binary
- Add steez preamble (coordinate with dot-dih)

### 2. Narrow install path for coworkers
- steez install browse should install browse + netsuite-browse together (they're one product)
- Or: standalone install script that doesn't require full steez CLI

### 3. Monorepo consumer script
- Add scripts/install-netsuite-browse.sh to monorepo
- Fetches latest browse binary + netsuite-browse SKILL.md from steez
- Coworkers run this instead of installing full steez

### 4. Remove old copy from monorepo
- Delete .claude/skills/netsuite-browse/ from monorepo
- Update monorepo docs to point to steez

## Key decisions
- netsuite-browse is generic (works for any NetSuite instance), so it belongs in the public repo
- Binary and skill should be versioned together
- Monorepo becomes a consumer, not an owner
notes:
## Open questions

**Analytics destination:** Should netsuite-browse skill usage log to the project repo (.steez/ or .beads/ in the monorepo) or to ~/.steez/analytics/ (personal local)? Argument for repo: all coworker usage is captured in one place, visible in retros. Argument for local: consistent with how all other steez skills log. Possibly both — local for personal tracking, repo for team visibility.

title: Port usage analytics to all skills
description:
Add JSONL usage logging to non-steez skills (jira, reminders, tmux, agenda, audit, investigate, loop-prompt, sharpen-skill, claude-spawn). Each invocation appends to ~/.steez/analytics/skill-usage.jsonl. Enables retrospectives and usage pattern analysis across all skills, not just steez ones.

NOTES
Decisions: (1) Keep steez's ungated writes (no telemetry opt-in, always log locally). (2) Restore gstack's .pending-* crash recovery pattern (orphaned start events get finalized on next skill run). (3) Skip remote telemetry (no upstream). (4) Port both start and end events to all non-steez skills. (5) End event is inline bash at skill completion, not a hook — best-effort but crash recovery mitigates.
notes: DECISION: PORT. Add JSONL usage logging to all skills (start event in preamble, end event at completion). Restore gstack's .pending-* crash recovery pattern — orphaned start events get finalized on next skill run. Keep steez's ungated writes (no telemetry opt-in, always log locally). Skip remote telemetry. /steez-retro reads this data for weekly summaries — without it, non-steez skills are invisible to retro.
close_reason: Skills are now edited directly. Voice and analytics are already in each skill's managed preamble.

title: feat: multi-session support for steez-browse (-s <name>)
description: steez-browse currently supports one daemon per project (one state file at .steez/browse.json). Need named sessions so multiple browser instances can run in parallel within the same project. Approach: each session gets its own state file (.steez/sessions/<name>/browse.json) and its own daemon process. ~100 lines estimated. playwright-cli has this via -s flag — steal the pattern, not the implementation.

title: Port unified voice to all skills
description: Add the steez voice directive to non-steez skills. Decide: inline the full block, or create a shared partial that skills reference. Voice covers tone, writing rules, concreteness standards, banned words.
notes:
DECISION: TIERED. Three tiers:
(1) FULL VOICE (Parts A+B+C): agenda, audit, sharpen-skill — make opinionated recommendations where identity, tone, and writing rules matter.
(2) WRITING RULES ONLY (Part C): jira, reminders, loop-prompt — CRUD/output tools that benefit from no AI slop and concreteness, but don't need CTO philosophy.
(3) SKIP: tmux, claude-spawn — almost no prose output, just bash commands.
Part A=identity/philosophy, Part B=tone/humor, Part C=writing rules (no em dashes, banned AI vocab, name specifics, end with action).

description: Add the ## AskUserQuestion Format section to steez skills. Every AskUserQuestion call re-grounds the user with project, branch, and current bead/task context. Adapted from gstack's always-on format (they had a conditional 3+ sessions mode called ELI16, removed it in v0.4.3 in favor of always-on). The session file tracking (mkdir/touch/find in preamble) is dead code in gstack — nothing reads it. Codex confirmed via full codebase search. Steez adaptation: include bead context in re-grounding (steez-original addition).

title: feat: tiered preamble system for steez skills
description: Implement a managed-block sync generator for steez skill preambles. Each SKILL.md keeps its hand-authored skill logic but has a delimited preamble section (<!-- BEGIN MANAGED PREAMBLE --> ... <!-- END MANAGED PREAMBLE -->) generated from shared source files based on preamble-tier frontmatter. Tiers control which sections each skill gets: T1 (session tracking, analytics, writing rules, completion status), T2 (T1 + full voice + AskUserQuestion format + completeness scoring), T3 (T2 + repo mode + search-before-building). A lightweight sync script assembles the preamble block per tier and a check mode validates freshness. Replaces the original hand-maintain approach after investigation showed drift already caused regressions (ELI16 lost in gstack fork). Codex independently recommended this managed-block pattern over both hand-maintain and full-file templates.
notes:
TIER ASSIGNMENTS (from investigation):
T1 (minimal — session tracking, analytics, writing rules, completion status): tmux, claude-spawn, jira, reminders, loop-prompt
T2 (+decision support — full voice, AskUserQuestion format, completeness scoring): agenda, audit
T3 (+context awareness — repo mode, search-before-building): sharpen-skill
Existing steez skills keep their current tier assignments (T1-T4 per gstack convention).
close_reason: Implemented tiered preamble system: 13 section templates in preamble/sections/, tier config in preamble/tiers.json, steez sync Go command with --check mode. 24 skills synced (16 existing + 8 newly tiered). Tests pass.

title: codex Stop hook: call agent-eventsd evidence
description:
Add a Codex Stop hook that calls `agent-eventsd evidence --state idle` for codex / ren-codex panes. Gated on the user's `codex_hooks=true` opt-in in `~/.codex/config.toml`.

Depends on steez-s4t (fake-codex must fire evidence in the primary-path test).

## Why

Codex parity for the fast-evidence path. Without this bead, codex panes still take 30s via degraded fallback even after the Claude hook wiring lands. Lower priority than Claude because:
- Codex hooks require an opt-in feature flag most users don't have enabled.
- Codex Stop firing semantics are less well-documented and rely on `async = true`.

## Scope

### New hook file: `shared/steez/hooks/codex-stop.sh`

Mirror of the Claude evidence path. Reads Codex Stop payload from stdin:
- Extract `session_id`, `transcript_path`.
- Gate on `[ -n "$TMUX_PANE" ]`.
- `cursor=$(wc -c < "$transcript_path" 2>/dev/null | tr -d ' ' || echo 0)`
- Fire-and-forget: `agent-eventsd evidence --pane "$TMUX_PANE" --state idle --transcript-cursor "$cursor" >/dev/null 2>&1 &`
- No sidecar write — Codex has no permission sidecar path today.

### `internal/installer/runtime_assets.go`

Add `codex-stop.sh` to `SharedCodexHookSymlinks()` so it's installed alongside the existing `session-start.sh` symlink.

### `specs/agent-events.md`

Minor addendum noting Codex Stop as the Codex-side fast-evidence producer (the Claude bead will have already added the general hook-producer paragraph).

### User-side registration (NOT installer-mutated)

Document in the hook file's header comment and in a README note:
- User sets `[features] codex_hooks = true` in `~/.codex/config.toml`.
- User adds Stop hook registration pointing at `$HOME/.codex/hooks/codex-stop.sh`.

Installer does not mutate `config.toml`.

## TDD

### Red

Add runtime test in `shared/steez/tests/agent/test-agent-eventsd-runtime.sh`:

Scenario `codex_hook_fires_evidence_and_notification_arrives_within_2s`:
- Spawn fake codex + spawner.
- `agent-send $target "hi"`.
- Drive fake to idle via fifo.
- Assert delivery lands within 2s, watch clears.

Will fail on HEAD (codex hook doesn't exist). With steez-s4t already landed, the fake fires evidence, so this test validates the end-to-end path rather than the hook wiring per se — the production value is that the hook IS the mechanism that fires evidence in real codex. The fake shortcut keeps the test zero-token.

### Green

Add `codex-stop.sh`. Add symlink entry. Update spec.

### Refactor

If `codex-stop.sh` and the Claude hook's evidence dispatch share >60%% of code, extract a helper to `shared/steez/hooks/_evidence-dispatch.sh` used by both.

## Files

- `shared/steez/hooks/codex-stop.sh` (new)
- `internal/installer/runtime_assets.go`
- `shared/steez/tests/agent/test-agent-eventsd-runtime.sh`
- `specs/agent-events.md`

## Acceptance

- New runtime test passes (<2s on codex pane).
- All existing tests stay green.
- `codex-stop.sh` gets installed as a symlink by the installer.
- Spec updated in the same commit.
close_reason: Landed codex-stop.sh fast-evidence hook with runtime test (<2s budget), installer symlink, and spec addendum. Fake-codex parity from steez-s4t verified — no gap. Pre-existing Go test failures (TestIntegration_DoctorAfterInstall, TestInstallSpecInstallsClaudeAndCodexSkills, TestInstallStarterExcludesDeprecatedPlanningSkills) are unrelated local-env issues (missing browse binary).

title: spawn-agent: add --workflow preset for verified completion gates
description:
Context: when spawning a ren agent to ship a bead, there's no standard way to require independent verification before close. Current behavior: the agent's prompt inlines whatever gates I remember to type. ns-dy0 shipped with agent-side commit+close, then needed retroactive /simplify + verifier + sandbox smoke — too late to reopen without reverting.

Proposal: two workflow presets on spawn.sh, selected at spawn time.

- --workflow default → existing behavior (no injected gate text). Agent implements, tests, builds, commits, closes bead.
- --workflow verified → appends a completion-protocol block to the prompt: implement + unit tests + build → /simplify pass → verifier subagent → sandbox smoke (when applicable) → commit → close bead. Close is gated on verifier PASS.

Axis is NOT 'simple vs complex' (vibes-based, argued every spawn). Axis IS 'does this change carry regression risk worth independent verification'. Caller picks at spawn time.

Mechanism: one-line flag parse in spawn.sh. Per-preset workflow text stored as a constant (or file) and appended to --prompt. No new machinery.

Avoid scope creep: resist a third 'dry-run' preset. If someone wants to eyeball the diff, they can read the pane.

Doc: update SKILL.md with the flag and the two presets. Example spawn calls for each.

Open question for implementation: where should the preset text live? Inline constants in spawn.sh or separate files under ~/.steez/repo/skills/spawn-agent/prompts/? Lean toward files — easier to edit without touching shell.

Related real-world trigger: netsuite-browse epic ns-vko (Dumak/netsuite-main bd). ns-dy0 exposed the gap that motivated this.
