title: Extend retro to create skill-finding beads from low-rated self-reports
description:
## Context

Parent bead: steez-31x (preamble audit) locked in that 8 steez skills will inline self-reports in their final user-facing message (investigate, qa, audit, cso, sharpen-skill, design-review, workshop, office-hours). Every run produces a rating + critique + "what would make this a 10", plus an optional "STEEZ FINDING: ..." line when the rating is below 8 and the problem is a steez tooling issue.

That's the producer side. This bead is the consumer side: extend the retro system so it reads session transcripts, finds those self-reports, and creates beads for the low-rated ones automatically. This closes the loop: skill surfaces issue → retro captures → bead created → future session fixes.

## What this bead does

Extend the retro prompt so the forked retro agent, in addition to its current job (writing a session summary to ~/.claude/analytics/prometheus/retros/), also:

1. Scans the session transcript for lines matching "STEEZ FINDING:" (the literal prefix the 8 target skills will emit when rating < 8 and issue is steez-side).
2. For each unique finding, creates a bead using bd create:
   - title: the finding text (stripped of the prefix)
   - type: task
   - priority: 3 (or 4 — TBD, see sub-decisions)
   - labels: skill-finding, auto-created-by-retro (or similar tagging TBD)
   - description: includes the source skill name, the full self-report section from the transcript, and a reference back to the session slug
3. Before creating, greps existing open beads with the skill-finding label for similar titles to avoid duplicates. If a match exists, appends a note to the existing bead instead ("seen again in session <slug>") rather than creating a new one.

## Target files

- **Primary**: ~/Projects/Personal/prometheus/hooks/retro-prompt.md — add the directive to the existing retro prompt
- **Secondary**: ~/Projects/Personal/prometheus/specs/retro.md — update the spec to reflect the new retro responsibility
- Possibly ~/Projects/Personal/prometheus/hooks/retro.sh if the shell script needs any changes to pass additional context to the forked agent (probably not, but check)

## Sub-decisions to resolve during implementation

1. **Threshold**: The producer side emits "STEEZ FINDING:" only when rating < 8 and issue is steez-side, so the retro doesn't need a separate threshold — it just acts on every STEEZ FINDING line it sees. Confirm this during implementation.
2. **Bead priority**: P3 default or P4? P4 keeps them out of bd ready until manually promoted, P3 makes them visible immediately. Lean P4 — model-generated findings are low-confidence signal.
3. **Bead type**: task, bug, or a custom type? task is the safe default. bug implies verified defect, which these aren't.
4. **Label scheme**: what labels to tag these with so they're filterable (e.g., bd list --label=skill-finding). Pick one or two labels, not a taxonomy.
5. **Dedup strategy**: string-match on title is brittle. Alternatives: fuzzy match, require exact match, or hash the finding text. Start with exact-match on stripped title and iterate if spam shows up.
6. **Max findings per retro**: cap at N per session to prevent bead spam if a skill fires 10 findings in one run. Probably 3-5.

## Done criteria

- Retro prompt updated with the directive
- Retro spec updated to document the new responsibility
- One live test: run a skill that will self-report low, let the session end, verify retro creates the expected bead
- All sub-decisions above resolved and documented in the bead notes
- Commit on main

## Blocked by

steez-31x must land first — the producer side (skill-inline self-reports) is the input this bead consumes.

title: docs: rewrite ARCHITECTURE.md layout and install-model sections
description:
# ARCHITECTURE.md structural rewrite — layout and deployment sections

## Problem

`ARCHITECTURE.md` has two sections that describe a system that doesn't exist anymore:

1. **"Where everything lives" → Repo (dotfiles) subsection, lines 15-33** — shows a directory tree rooted at `dotfiles/claude/.claude/skills/` with `steez-*` prefixed skill directories. This is the old gstack stow-based dotfiles layout that the steez fork doesn't use. The actual steez layout is:

   ```
   /Users/<user>/Projects/Personal/steez/
   ├── shared/steez/bin/           # 8 helper scripts
   ├── shared/steez/browse/        # headless browser (Playwright + Bun compiled binary)
   ├── shared/steez/hooks/         # session-start + skill-analytics hooks
   ├── skills/{name}/SKILL.md      # 22 skill directories
   ├── preamble/                   # managed preamble system (audit scope)
   ├── cmd/steez/                  # Go CLI entrypoint
   ├── internal/                   # Go packages (installer, config, tui, updater)
   ├── ETHOS.md / README.md / CLAUDE.md / ARCHITECTURE.md / FORK_MANIFEST.md
   └── skills.json                 # skill manifest + categories + profiles
   ```

2. **"Stow deployment" subsection, lines 51-57** — describes a stow-based deployment model where `~/.claude/skills/` is a symlink to `dotfiles/claude/.claude/skills/` via directory folding. This is completely wrong. The actual steez install model per `CLAUDE.md`:
   - Skills installed as per-file symlinks: `~/.claude/skills/{name}` → `repo/skills/{name}/`
   - Shared runtime via installer-managed symlinks under `~/.steez/bin/` and `~/.steez/repo`
   - Install registry at `~/.steez/installed.json`
   - Updates are live mutation: `git pull` in-place, symlinks already point at checkout
   - No stow, no directory folding, no dotfiles package

## Scope

- Rewrite the `### Repo (dotfiles)` subsection (lines 15-33) to show the actual steez layout
- Rewrite the `### Stow deployment` subsection (lines 51-57) to describe the Go CLI installer model
- Rename `### Stow deployment` to something accurate (e.g., `### Install model`)
- Keep the `### Runtime (~/.steez/)` subsection (lines 35-49) as-is for now — it references `sessions/`, `eureka.jsonl`, `skill-reports/` which are in the scope of `steez-31x` (preamble audit) and will be updated when that lands
- Preserve the section's role in the doc: explaining *why* the layout is the way it is, not just *what* it is

## Why this was deferred

Spotted during the docs cleanup pass after `steez-ts9`. The trivial rot (counts, dead skill references, data flow diagram) was fixed in commit `9fc4aa1`. The layout and install-model rewrites are non-trivial — they require accurately describing a different system from scratch, and bundling them into a doc-hygiene commit would have made the diff hard to review. This bead exists so the remaining structural rot doesn't get lost.

## Definition of done

- `ARCHITECTURE.md` "Where everything lives" section accurately describes the steez repo layout (not gstack dotfiles)
- `ARCHITECTURE.md` "Stow deployment" section renamed and rewritten to describe the Go CLI installer model (per-file symlinks, `~/.steez/installed.json` registry, `git pull` update mechanism)
- Cross-referenced against `CLAUDE.md` project architecture section for consistency
- No references remain to `dotfiles/claude/.claude/skills/`, `stow`, `directory folding`, or `steez-*` prefixed skill directories (except in the `### Key differences from gstack` fork-comparison table, where they are correct historical documentation)

## Related beads

- **steez-31x** — preamble audit. Will update the `### Runtime (~/.steez/)` subsection as part of its own work. This bead should coordinate with 31x rather than overlap.
- **steez-ts9** — /workshop skill implementation. The docs cleanup pass that spawned this bead was a follow-up to ts9.
close_reason: Rewrote ARCHITECTURE.md 'Where everything lives' and 'Stow deployment' sections to reflect the actual steez install model (Go CLI + per-skill symlinks + ~/.steez/installed.json registry). Also fixed adjacent dotfiles/stow rot caught by the DoD: comparison table rows, Adding a new skill flow, Porting from gstack step 2 (steez-* prefix), Updating from upstream commands, and three rationale paragraphs calling steez 'deployed from dotfiles'. Cross-checked against CLAUDE.md. Runtime subsection left untouched per coordination with steez-31x. Commit 2e0a359.

title: workshop: bd start auto-trigger? (verification)
description:
# workshop verification dogfood

**Note:** This bead is the verification dogfood for steez-ts9 (the /workshop skill implementation). It is a real workshop session on a real fuzzy question, but it also serves as the end-to-end lifecycle test required by the steez-ts9 definition of done. Future `bd list --label=workshop` filters can identify this bead by its title prefix and the "(verification)" tag.

## Originating question

While implementing /workshop, a fuzzy question surfaced that wasn't covered explicitly in the spec:

> Should `bd start <workshop-bead-id>` automatically trigger the /workshop skill when the bead has `--label=workshop`, or should the user invoke /workshop explicitly?

The skill's "Resuming a paused workshop" section assumes the agent is *already* in /workshop mode when the bead resumes. But nothing forces that to be true. If the user just types `bd start <id>` on a workshop bead in a fresh session, the agent has no signal to enter workshop mode unless something hooks the label.

## Why workshop is the right tool

This is a fuzzy architectural question with multiple plausible answers, no existing precedent in steez (skills auto-trigger from description matching, not from bead labels), and real carry-cost implications (any hook is infrastructure to maintain). It's exactly the kind of "I'm not sure but..." input workshop is for.
design:
## OPEN

None.

## RESOLVED

### [Q] Should `bd start <id>` on a workshop-labeled bead auto-trigger the /workshop skill?

A: No. Don't add auto-trigger infrastructure. Three lenses converged on this: (1) **Carry cost** — an auto-trigger needs a hook to detect `label=workshop` on `bd start`, and that hook is infrastructure to maintain, test, and avoid false-positives on. (2) **Landscape check** — steez has zero precedent for label-driven skill invocation. Skills auto-trigger from the description field via Claude Code's native mechanism. The closest analog is `bd prime`, which loads context but doesn't invoke a skill. Adding label-driven invocation would be a new paradigm running alongside the existing one. (3) **Pre-mortem** — six months later, the auto-trigger fires when the user just wants to read or update a workshop bead without entering workshop mode, or it fires during automated `bd start` calls from `agent-spawn` and pollutes orchestrated sessions. The existing description-field trigger handles resume invocation: when the user says anything matching the workshop trigger phrases ("let me run something by you", "want to chew on this", etc.), /workshop activates via Claude Code's native mechanism. The "Resuming a paused workshop" section in the SKILL.md is correct as-is and requires no changes.

## KILLED

description:
# /workshop — thinking-partner skill

## Problem

steez has no skill for the moment *before* an idea is an idea.

`/office-hours` is heavyweight product ideation — it asks "should I build this product?" and produces a design doc that chains into plan reviews and `/ship`. It assumes you have a thesis you want to validate.

`/workshop` sits *upstream* of office-hours. Its job is to chew on a half-formed thought until it's clear enough to either become work, become a memory entry, be killed with a reason, or (rarely) graduate to office-hours as a real product idea. Most workshop sessions produce a single implementation bead or a kill — office-hours graduation is the rare path.

The failure mode it prevents: empty sessions where the user has a hunch, the agent has nothing to grip, and the session dies producing nothing. Evidence the gap is real: 4 of 6 recent non-trivial sessions would have been meaningfully better with workshop existing (pro-doctor-implementation, obsidian-vault-linking, unpaired-wakes-investigation, empty-qa-session). See notes for the pressure-test table.

## When workshop is NOT needed

Workshop is **not** a universal entry point. It has a cost — it applies lenses, maintains a Q&A log, runs a disposal walk, produces a bead. Imposing that on a scoped task is exactly the over-engineering workshop is supposed to prevent.

Skip workshop and go straight to `bd create` + implementation when:
- The task is fully scoped ("add --verbose flag to foo")
- You've already validated the idea elsewhere
- It's a bug to fix → use `/investigate`
- It's code to review → use `/codex`
- It's a committed product to validate → use `/office-hours` directly

Use workshop when:
- You have a hunch but don't know if it's worth pursuing
- You have an idea but the approach is unclear
- You notice a pattern and don't know what to do about it
- You're chewing on architectural decisions
- You're not sure if this is one thing or three things

Workshop is for *fuzzy* inputs. Clear inputs bypass it.

## Architecture: bead-only

One bead per workshop session. No separate doc files, no project index files, no parking-lot files. The bead carries everything:

- **description field** — originating question + framing
- **design field** — structured Q&A log (open / resolved / killed sections)
- **notes field** — disposal results, cross-project items, related context

Cross-project dumps mid-workshop go in the workshop bead's notes with a `[cross-project: <target>]` marker. No global parking-lot file. Past workshops are discoverable via `bd list --label=workshop`. `bd list` IS the index.

### Three lifecycle states

- **active** — currently being worked on (in_progress)
- **paused** — open, has Q&A log, waiting to resume via `bd start <id>` in any future session
- **disposed** — closed via the disposal walk, with reason

A workshop is bound to one bead, NOT to one Claude Code session. Context fills up? Hand off via `bd start <id>` in a fresh session. Take a break? Resume tomorrow. Same primitive (`bd start`) is used for plan-mode handoff at disposal — one mechanism, two intents.

### Cold-start completeness rule

Every Q&A log entry must stand alone. No "earlier in the conversation" references. A fresh session reading the bead has to be able to pick up where the last session left off. This is the hardest discipline to maintain and the most load-bearing.

## The five lenses (workshop's stance)

These are the forcing questions workshop applies to whatever the user brings in. **Not a checklist** — lenses on a bench, picked as relevant to the thread being chewed. Multiple lenses can fire on a single thread.

1. **XY check** — what is the user *actually* asking, underneath the words? Catches: solving the literal question instead of the real one. Maps to the "XY problem" — asking about implementation Y when the real problem is X.

2. **Carry cost** — what does this commit us to *carrying* in code, cognition, and attention? Catches speculative complexity. The post-AI reframe of YAGNI: build cost has cratered thanks to AI, but maintenance cost and cognitive load have not. Every line of code still has to be read, understood, debugged, held in someone's head. Don't add what you won't use — not because building is expensive, but because every unused thing has to be *carried*.

3. **Pre-mortem** — it's six months later and this was a disaster. What killed it? Catches blind spots and assumptions doing all the work. Maps to Gary Klein's pre-mortem technique. Research shows pre-mortems surface ~30% more risks than standard risk reviews because imagining failure as settled fact reveals concerns people won't raise when it's "just a risk."

4. **Landscape check** — who has already solved this, and why aren't we using their answer? Catches reinventing poorly. Maps to ETHOS Layer 1 (tried-and-true) / Layer 2 (new-and-popular) / Layer 3 (first-principles) applied at design time. If prior art exists, the reason for NOT using it must be explicit.

5. **Smallest disprover** — what's the cheapest test that would prove the central premise wrong? Catches workshop confidence outpacing evidence. Maps to build-measure-learn applied to thinking, not product. Before committing to an approach, find the minimum experiment that disproves it.

These are the workshop's voice. The agent picks lenses opportunistically based on which one is most useful for the thread on the bench.

## Core rule: agent owns bookkeeping, user owns thinking

The workshop skill MUST keep the open / resolved / killed lists updated **continuously and silently**, in real time, as the conversation moves. It does NOT ask the user to validate bookkeeping. If the agent thinks something is resolved and it isn't, that's the agent's error to fix, not the user's job to catch.

Specifically:
- Moving a thread from **open → resolved** happens silently the moment the agent and user land on a resolution
- Moving a thread from **open → killed** happens silently the moment they decide not to pursue it, with the reason captured
- Adding a new **open thread** happens the moment the user surfaces a question, even if off-thread or off-topic
- The user can correct the lists if they notice something wrong, but that's the exception, not the workflow

The only moments the user enters the bookkeeping loop:
- **Mode A invocation** — agent presents the current state. User reads, decides what to chew on next. No validation asked.
- **Mode B invocation** — agent presents disposal groups (not per-thread) and walks each one with disposal options. Resolved and killed entries are NOT re-validated.

**Violating this rule defeats the entire purpose of the skill.** Workshop exists to *offload* cognitive overhead from the user. Asking the user to validate bookkeeping re-imposes the overhead workshop was supposed to remove.

## Disposal walk (Mode B) — session-level grouping

At session end, the agent reads all open threads and **groups them into natural disposal units**. Most workshop sessions produce 1-2 groups, rarely 3+, never one-group-per-open-question. The agent's job is to recognize what the open threads collectively point at and present disposals for the groups, not for each individual thread.

### Six possible disposals per group

1. **Bead → straight implement** (most common). The work is small, atomic, single-session, <3 files, no decomposition needed. Spawn a bead with the implementation spec, link via `bd dep add` if needed, close workshop bead with reason.

2. **Bead → plan mode → atomic beads → implement**. Multi-component work with real architectural decisions, dependencies between pieces, or multi-session scope. Create a bead, instruction in notes: "next session: `bd start <id>`, enter plan mode, this bead is the anchor." Plan mode decomposes it into atomic implementation beads.

3. **Memory entry**. The output is a behavioral rule or identity insight, not a thing to build. Write to `~/.claude/agent-memory/prometheus/`, update MEMORY.md index, close workshop bead with reason. The insight changes how Prometheus operates, not what code exists.

4. **Kill**. Workshop revealed the idea was bad, redundant, or solving a non-problem. Close workshop bead with reason that captures *why* it was killed. The killed section of the design field is sacred — it stops future workshops from re-litigating decisions.

5. **Office-hours handoff** (rare). Workshop revealed this is actually a new product idea with external users and PMF questions. Close with reason "graduating to /office-hours" and run office-hours. Only fires when the remaining questions are demand/wedge/customer/future-fit questions that workshop's lenses don't answer.

6. **Carry** (non-disposal). Lenses fired but no resolution emerged. Out of time/energy/context. The bead stays open in "paused" state. Picked up next workshop session via `bd start <id>` in any future session.

### When does each disposal fire

| Disposal | Trigger | Signal |
|---|---|---|
| Bead → implement | Small, scoped, atomic | One sentence to describe, <3 files, no architectural decisions |
| Bead → plan mode → beads | Multi-component with decisions/dependencies | Multiple files/sessions, real architectural choices, decomposition adds value |
| Memory | Output is a rule, not a thing | Changes operation, not code |
| Kill | Idea is bad/redundant/solved | Carry-cost or landscape-check fired hard |
| Office-hours | Real product for external users | Remaining questions are about demand/wedge/customer |
| Carry | Didn't converge | Lenses fired, no resolution |

"Nothing" is a first-class outcome. Most thoughts shouldn't survive scrutiny. If workshop always pressures toward an artifact-creating disposal, it'll manufacture fake work. The kill path is sacred.

## Mode A vs Mode B (loose-ends behavior, baked into workshop)

Both modes use the same view-generation logic: **open / resolved (collapsed by default) / killed (always shown with reasons)**.

### Mode A — review only

User asks "any open threads?", "what's still open?", "loose ends?", "loose-ends". Workshop presents the view. User reads, decides what to chew on next. Nothing else happens. Useful mid-session for taking stock without ending.

### Mode B — review and dispose

User says "let's wrap", "let's land this", "let's dispose", "we're done". Workshop groups the open threads into disposal units, presents each group with its recommended disposal, walks them with the user. Resolved and killed entries are NOT re-presented.

The killed section is load-bearing. Always show it with reasons. Stops re-litigation of things already decided against.

## Voice and preamble

**Voice:** senior engineer who's been burned. Matches the lens set's risk-skewed bias (carry cost, pre-mortem, smallest disprover are all "what could go wrong" lenses). Inherits Prometheus voice — does NOT redefine.

**Preamble:** none. Born clean per `steez-31x` audit direction. No Voice block, no Completeness Principle, no AskUserQuestion format block, no Self-Report, no Completion Status Protocol, no Plan Status Footer, no proactive flag check, no PID file touch, no decorative variable echoes. Workshop is the post-audit reference for what a slim skill looks like.

## Triggering

Skills auto-trigger from the description field — that's the native Claude Code mechanism. Workshop's description should match phrases like:

- "I have a thought"
- "what if we"
- "could we"
- "I'm wondering"
- "I'm not sure but"
- "help me think through"
- "is this overengineered"
- "something feels off"
- "let me run something by you"
- "want to chew on this"
- "is this a good idea"
- "should we even..."

Locked candidate list. Implementation session refines the final wording when writing the SKILL.md description field.

## Workshop vs office-hours (why they're separate skills)

| | Office-hours | Workshop |
|---|---|---|
| Input assumption | "I have an idea to validate" | "I don't know if this is even an idea" |
| Output | Design doc + plan review chain | Bead with disposal options (kill is valid) |
| Stance | Product-market validation (6 forcing questions) | Decision-making (5 lenses) |
| Weight | Heavy (6+ phases, mandatory alternatives, cross-model check) | Light (conversational, lens-driven) |
| Handoff | `/plan-ceo-review`, `/plan-eng-review`, `/ship` | bead → implement (usually) |

They occupy genuinely different niches. Workshop's lens overlap with office-hours is thin — only "pre-mortem ≈ premise challenge" and "landscape check ≈ landscape awareness." Office-hours's six forcing questions are product-specific; workshop's five lenses are general decision questions.

The `/workshop → /office-hours` graduation path exists but is rare. Most workshops end in bead or kill, not office-hours.

## Design field template (locked schema for Q&A log)

```
## OPEN
### [Q] <question text>
Surfaced: <turn or moment>
Status: <one-line current state>

## RESOLVED
### [Q] <question text>
A: <one-paragraph resolution>

## KILLED
### [killed] <question text>
Reason: <why this was killed>
```

Implementation session can refine markdown formatting if a cleaner structure emerges, but this is the v1 schema.

## bd command sequencing (locked v1 contract)

| Phase | Command |
|---|---|
| Session start | `bd create --type=task --priority=3 --label=workshop --title="<derived>" --body-file=<initial framing> --design-file=<empty Q&A template>` |
| Mid-session updates | `bd update <id> --design="$(cat <updated log>)"` after each resolution/kill |
| Cross-project dump | `bd update <id> --append-notes="[cross-project: <target>] <content>"` |
| Disposal: kill | `bd close <id> --reason="<why killed>"` |
| Disposal: child bead | `bd create --parent=<workshop-id> ...` then `bd update <workshop-id> --append-notes="spawned <child-id>: <thread>"` |
| Disposal: plan-mode handoff | `bd update <id> --append-notes="HANDOFF: bd start <id> in fresh Prometheus session, enter plan mode"` |

Implementation session can revise if a cleaner pattern emerges, but this is the v1 contract.

## Definition of done

- `skills/workshop/SKILL.md` exists and is registered in `skills.json` with an appropriate category
- Workshop creates a bead at session start with `--label=workshop` and a structured design field
- The agent maintains the open/resolved/killed lists silently and continuously per the core rule
- Mode A presents the current state without asking for validation
- Mode B groups open threads at the session level and presents disposal options per group
- The workshop bead can be paused mid-session and resumed via `bd start <id>` in a fresh session with no continuity loss
- At least one real workshop session has been run end-to-end successfully under the new skill
- The skill description field triggers on natural language patterns matching half-formed thinking

## Related beads

- **steez-31x** — preamble audit. Workshop is the greenfield post-audit reference for what a slim skill looks like.
- **steez-d84** — skill telemetry gaps. Relevant if workshop adds analytics writes (it currently doesn't).

## Handoff instruction

```
cd /Users/stevedimakos/Projects/Personal/steez
bd start <this-bead-id>
```

The bead's description and design field together are the complete implementation spec. Read them, write `skills/workshop/SKILL.md` per the spec, add an entry to `skills.json`, run one real end-to-end workshop session to verify the lifecycle, close the bead with `bd close <id> --reason="..."`.

**No plan mode required** — this bead IS the implementation contract.

# Workshop Q&A log: design /workshop skill

**Session:** 2026-04-07
**Agent:** Prometheus
**Note:** This Q&A log is itself a dogfood of the format. The workshop skill does not yet exist; this session designed it manually by applying the five lenses through conversation. The carry-cost lens fired four times during this session, catching real over-engineering on the skill's own design. That meta-validation is part of why the lens set is locked as-is.

## OPEN

None. All threads resolved or killed by session end.

## RESOLVED

### [Q] Is there a gap in steez skills that a thinking-partner skill could fill, or does /office-hours already cover this?

A: Yes, there's a real gap. /office-hours is heavyweight product ideation and assumes you have a thesis to validate. The gap is for half-formed thoughts upstream of that — the moment before you even know whether you have an idea. /workshop fills that niche. Evidence: 4 of 6 recent non-trivial sessions would have been meaningfully better with workshop existing (pro-doctor-implementation, obsidian-vault-linking, unpaired-wakes-investigation, empty-qa-session).

### [Q] Should the workshop skill produce an artifact, or just be a thinking mode?

A: Artifact required. The user dumps thoughts mid-stream and asks "any open threads?" at session end. Without an artifact, threads evaporate. A thinking mode with no persistence would hurt this user's specific workflow.

### [Q] Should the artifact be a markdown file, a bead, or something else?

A: Bead-only. Initial instinct was a markdown file based on prior art (brainstorm-plan, robertguss, superpowers all use files). The carry-cost lens fired on this: beads already provide description, design, and notes fields. Inventing a new file convention was duplicating existing infrastructure. One bead per workshop session carries everything.

### [Q] What's the atomic unit of the Q&A log — ideas or questions?

A: Questions (Q-as-atom). This matches the user's specific workflow of dumping questions mid-conversation. Prior art treats ideas as the unit; workshop sharpens it to questions because they're more concrete and more aligned with how the user thinks.

### [Q] Should AskUserQuestion calls the agent makes to the user also be logged in the Q&A log?

A: No. AskUserQuestion is workshop *machinery* (used for Mode B disposal walks and forced choices), not substantive thinking. The Q&A log captures substantive thinking only. Machinery and thinking don't overlap in the log.

### [Q] What's the workshop's stance — what forcing questions does it apply?

A: Five lenses. (1) XY check — what is the user actually asking? (2) Carry cost — the post-AI YAGNI reframe. (3) Pre-mortem — it's six months later and this died, why? (4) Landscape check — who solved this already? (5) Smallest disprover — cheapest test that proves the premise wrong. Each maps to established framework lineage. Not a checklist; lenses on a bench, picked opportunistically.

### [Q] What's the skill name?

A: /workshop. Carries the right "two of us at a bench" energy. Rejected /unpack (purely analytical, misses iterative side) and /pick-my-brain (implies one-directional interrogation).

### [Q] What voice should workshop use?

A: Senior engineer who's been burned. Matches the lens set's risk-skewed bias (carry cost, pre-mortem, smallest disprover are all "what could go wrong" lenses). Inherits Prometheus voice — does NOT redefine.

### [Q] What's the preamble strategy?

A: Born preamble-clean (option a). No Voice block, no Completeness Principle, no AskUserQuestion format block, no Self-Report, no Completion Status Protocol, no Plan Status Footer, no proactive flag check, no decorative variable echoes. Workshop is the post-steez-31x-audit reference for what a slim skill looks like.

### [Q] How does the skill auto-trigger on user input?

A: Skills auto-trigger from the description field — that's the native Claude Code mechanism. Workshop's description matches phrases like "I have a thought", "what if we", "could we", etc. No separate trigger logic, no proactive flag check, no keyword parsing in code. The description field IS the trigger.

### [Q] Is a workshop session bound to one Claude Code session?

A: No. Workshop is bound to one bead, not one CC session. Three lifecycle states: active, paused, disposed. Context fills up? Hand off via `bd start <id>` in a fresh session. Take a break? Resume tomorrow. Same primitive (`bd start`) is used for plan-mode handoff at disposal — one mechanism, two intents. Q&A log must be cold-start complete so a fresh session can pick up where the last one left off.

### [Q] How does the workshop hand off to plan mode at disposal (when the disposal is plan mode)?

A: Via the bead itself. Keep workshop bead open, append-notes instruction: "next session: `bd start <id>`, enter plan mode, this bead is the anchor." Plan mode reads the workshop bead's description and design field, decomposes into atomic implementation beads, exits. No separate plan file, no new file types.

### [Q] Should loose-ends tracking be a separate skill or built into workshop?

A: Built into workshop. Two modes: Mode A (review only — user asks "any open threads?", agent shows current state, nothing else) and Mode B (review + dispose — user says "let's wrap", agent groups open threads and walks disposal). Same view-generation logic, different post-view actions. Cross-session generic version (scanning any conversation for ambient loose ends) was killed as scope creep and unsolvable with unstructured input.

### [Q] Are we dogfooding this session as a workshop?

A: Yes. This entire session IS a workshop session, run manually because the skill doesn't exist yet. The Q&A log, the lens applications, the kills, the disposal walk — all of it is the format being proven on real use before being encoded into a SKILL.md. The carry-cost lens firing four times on the skill's own design is meta-validation that the lens set is real.

### [Q] Who owns the bookkeeping — the agent or the user?

A: The agent owns the bookkeeping. The user owns the thinking. The open/resolved/killed lists are maintained silently and continuously by the agent. The user is never asked to validate what's already been decided. They only enter the bookkeeping loop in Mode A (they read, they decide what to chew on next) and Mode B (they decide disposals, not revisit old decisions). If the agent miscategorizes something, that's the agent's error to fix, not the user's job to catch. Violating this rule defeats the purpose of the skill — workshop exists to offload cognitive overhead, not to re-impose it in the form of validation questions.

### [Q] What are the description trigger phrases for auto-triggering /workshop?

A: Locked candidate list: "I have a thought", "what if we", "could we", "I'm wondering", "I'm not sure but", "help me think through", "is this overengineered", "something feels off", "let me run something by you", "want to chew on this", "is this a good idea", "should we even..." Implementation session refines final wording.

### [Q] What's the template for the bead's design field (Q&A log)?

A: Locked markdown schema with three sections (OPEN / RESOLVED / KILLED). Each Q entry has standalone Q text, surfaced marker, and status/answer/reason depending on section. See description field for locked schema.

### [Q] What's the bd command sequencing for the workshop lifecycle?

A: Locked v1 contract: session start uses `bd create` with `--label=workshop`, mid-session updates use `bd update --design`, cross-project dumps use `bd update --append-notes`, disposal uses `bd close --reason` or `bd create --parent` for child beads. See description field for locked command table.

### [Q] Does the workshop bead need plan mode before implementation?

A: No. The workshop bead IS the implementation contract. Plan mode's value is when you need to decompose a large piece of work into atomic pieces. The workshop skill is a single-file skill creation — one SKILL.md file, one skills.json entry, one end-to-end test. No decomposition needed. Skip plan mode, go straight to implementation. This was the fourth carry-cost lens firing in the session.

### [Q] Should workshop be merged into office-hours or stay as a separate skill?

A: Stay separate. Office-hours is heavyweight product ideation with product-specific forcing questions (demand, wedge, PMF). Workshop is lightweight decision-making with general lenses. Lens overlap is thin (2 out of 11 total). Different stances, different outputs (design doc vs bead), different assumptions. Forcing half-formed thoughts through office-hours's 6-phase ceremony is exactly the over-engineering workshop is supposed to prevent.

### [Q] Does building workshop require modifying any existing skills?

A: No. Workshop is additive. Its outputs (beads) flow through existing bead infrastructure, not through the office-hours/plan-review pipeline. Office-hours doesn't need to know about workshop. The workshop → office-hours handoff path is invoked explicitly by the user in the rare case a workshop reveals a real product idea.

### [Q] Should /workshop be the universal entry point for all tasks?

A: No. Workshop is for *fuzzy* inputs — hunches, unclear approaches, architectural uncertainty. Clear inputs (scoped features, defined bugs, committed product ideas) should skip workshop and go directly to the appropriate existing skill or `bd create`. Workshop has cost; imposing it on every task is over-engineering. The filter at the front: "Is the work scoped and clear? If yes, skip workshop. If no, /workshop."

### [Q] Does the disposal walk iterate one disposal per open thread, or per group?

A: Per group, not per thread. The agent reads all open threads at session end and identifies natural disposal groups — usually 1, sometimes 2, rarely 3. One disposal decision per group, not per open question. A workshop with 8 open questions usually produces 1-2 disposals, not 8 beads. The agent's job is to recognize what the open threads collectively point at, group them intelligently, and present disposals for the groups.

## KILLED

### [killed] Workshop produces a separate .md doc file

Reason: beads carry everything (description + design + notes). The carry-cost lens fired on the skill's own design — I was inventing infrastructure that already exists. Recursive validation of the lens set itself. First carry-cost fire of the session.

### [killed] Project index file (`~/.steez/projects/{slug}/workshops/_index.md`)

Reason: `bd list --label=workshop` IS the index. Structurally queryable, no separate file convention to maintain.

### [killed] Cross-project parking-lot file (`~/.steez/parking-lot.md`)

Reason: `bd remember` handles per-project insights. Cross-project items live in the workshop bead's notes with a `[cross-project: <target>]` marker. No global file needed.

### [killed] Separate /loose-ends skill that scans any session

Reason: Scope creep + the "judge what's open in unstructured history" problem is real and hard. Workshop-internal version uses the structured Q&A log, is much simpler, and matches the actual use case. Second carry-cost fire.

### [killed] Preamble bloat (Voice block, Completeness Principle, AskUserQuestion format block, Skill Self-Report, Completion Status Protocol, Plan Status Footer)

Reason: Born clean per steez-31x audit direction. Workshop inherits Prometheus voice. These sections are dead weight in the new skill context. Third implicit carry-cost fire via the audit.

### [killed] Plan mode for this bead

Reason: Plan mode's value is decomposition of large work. Workshop skill implementation is one SKILL.md file + one skills.json entry — no decomposition needed. The bead IS the implementation contract. Fresh session can go straight to implementation via `bd start`. Fourth carry-cost fire.

### [killed] Merging workshop into office-hours

Reason: Genuinely different stances, outputs, and weight. Office-hours is heavyweight product ideation; workshop is lightweight decision-making. Lens overlap is thin. Merging would either bloat office-hours or bury workshop in unnecessary ceremony.

### [killed] /workshop as universal entry point for all tasks

Reason: Workshop has cost (lens application, Q&A log, disposal walk, bead creation). Imposing it on scoped tasks is over-engineering. Workshop is for fuzzy inputs only. Clear inputs skip it and go directly to `bd create` or the appropriate existing skill.

### [killed] Per-open-thread disposal walk

Reason: Creates bead inflation (8 open questions → 8 beads). Also imposes decision fatigue on the user. Disposal operates at the session level with natural grouping, not per open thread.

### [killed] Asking the user to validate the bookkeeping

Reason: Defeats the entire purpose of workshop. Workshop exists to offload cognitive overhead. Asking the user to validate open/resolved/killed categorizations re-imposes the load the skill was supposed to remove. Agent owns bookkeeping, user owns thinking. This became the "core rule" in the description field.

|---|---|---|
| pro-doctor-implementation | high | carry cost + smallest disprover |

## Synthesis
No single existing framework bundles workshop's 5 lenses into a session structure. Closest neighbor is "executive coaching with opinions" — content-skewed, peer-energy, position-taking. The lens set itself is novel even though individual lenses (XY problem, pre-mortem, etc.) are established.

- **steez-31x** [P2 OPEN] — preamble audit. Workshop is the greenfield post-audit reference for what a slim skill looks like. Workshop's born-clean design gives steez-31x a concrete target to converge toward.
- **steez-d84** [P2 OPEN] — skill telemetry gaps. Relevant if workshop adds analytics writes; currently it doesn't.

# Handoff instruction for the next session

```

```

The bead's description and design field together are the complete implementation spec. Read them, write `skills/workshop/SKILL.md` per the spec, add an entry to `skills.json`, run one real end-to-end workshop session to verify the lifecycle, close the bead with `bd close <id> --reason="..."`.

**No plan mode required** — this bead IS the implementation contract.

title: Stable symlink layer for skill scripts under ~/.steez/scripts/
description:
## Problem

Skill docs reference helper scripts (like jira_worklog.py) by absolute path. When skills migrated from ~/.claude/skills/ to ~/Projects/personal/steez/skills/, the paths broke silently. We caught this today when /steez-jira couldn't find jira_worklog.py. Hardcoding the new path works but is brittle... next migration breaks it again.

## Proposal

Create a stable symlink directory at ~/.steez/scripts/ that skill docs always reference. The symlinks point to wherever the actual scripts live in the source repo.

Example:
~/.steez/scripts/jira_worklog.py → ~/Projects/personal/steez/skills/jira/scripts/jira_worklog.py
~/.steez/scripts/jira_forms.py → ~/Projects/personal/steez/skills/jira/scripts/jira_forms.py

Skill docs would reference $HOME/.steez/scripts/jira_worklog.py, which never changes.

## Open Questions (investigate before implementing)

1. **What controls the symlinks?** Should this be part of `steez sync`, a new `steez install` step, or a Makefile target? Need to understand the existing install/sync flow first.
2. **When do symlinks get created/updated?** On every sync? Only on install? What about new scripts added later?
3. **Naming collisions** — if two skills have a script with the same name, flat ~/.steez/scripts/ won't work. Do we need ~/.steez/scripts/{skill-name}/ subdirectories instead?
4. **Discovery** — how does the symlink setup know which scripts to link? Convention (every file in skills/*/scripts/)? Explicit manifest in each skill?
5. **Existing references** — audit all SKILL.md files for hardcoded script paths that need updating to the new stable path.
6. **~/.steez/bin/ precedent** — browse and other binaries already live under ~/.steez/bin/. Should scripts go there too, or is scripts/ a separate namespace for a reason (bin = compiled/built, scripts = source)?

## Recommendation

Use ~/.steez/scripts/{skill-name}/ to avoid collisions. Wire it into whatever sync mechanism already manages ~/.steez/bin/. Convention-based: any file in skills/*/scripts/ gets linked automatically.

title: Investigate analytics hook support for context: fork skills
description:
The PostToolUse hook (shared/steez/hooks/skill-analytics.sh) does not fire when a skill has `context: fork` in its frontmatter. This blocks us from using `context: fork` for genuine isolation cases (different operating mode, true sub-context) without losing observability into which skills run and how often.

Current state: We are removing the heavy preamble pattern from all steez skills as a separate effort. That eliminates the need for `context: fork` as a workaround for persona contamination — lean skills run fine in main context and the existing PostToolUse hook continues to work for them.

Future goal: When we want `context: fork` for legitimate isolation use cases (not as a contamination workaround), we need an analytics path that survives the fork.

Investigation paths to explore:
1. Verify whether PreToolUse fires on forked Skill invocations — PostToolUse may fail because the result envelope differs after a fork.
2. Check whether a different hook event (e.g., SubagentStop) fires on fork completion.
3. Test inline self-reporting from inside the forked context: a single jq write to ~/.steez/analytics/skill-usage.jsonl from the skill body itself (filesystem is shared across contexts, so the write should land).
4. Read Claude Code's actual hook firing semantics for forked Skill tool invocations.

Outcome: A documented pattern (hook config update or skill template change) that lets a `context: fork` skill produce the same analytics record shape as a normal skill, so existing consumers of skill-usage.jsonl do not have to know which path was taken.

Priority: backlog. Not blocking any current work.

The upstream source is a single ~400-line Bun script at gstack/bin/gstack-global-discover.ts — no build step, no dependencies. Port requires renaming gstack→steez references and dropping it in steez/bin/.

value: agent-state distinguishes prometheus from claude via 'ps -E -p <claude_pid> | grep PROMETHEUS_SESSION=1' — direct env read on the claude process. Works because the prometheus wrapper exec's claude with that env var, and tmux split-window does NOT propagate caller env to new pane shells (new shells inherit from tmux SERVER env, not client env, unless update-environment is configured). So vanilla claude spawned from inside a prometheus session does not inherit the beacon. agent-history must internally normalize prometheus->claude for parsing since they share transcript format, but should preserve 'prometheus' as the display name for output.

tmux display-message -t <nonexistent-pane> -p 'fmt\twith\ttabs' returns the format skeleton with EMPTY field values separated by the literal tab characters (not empty string, not an error, exit 0). Example: '#{pane_id}\t#{pane_pid}\t#{pane_title}\t#{pane_current_path}' on pane %999 returns '\t\t\t'. A '[[ -z "$result" ]]' check on the raw output will falsely pass. Fix: parse the tab-separated fields first, THEN check if the parsed pane_id (or any required field) is empty. This bit me in agent-state during the /simplify refactor — shipped a wrong error message before catching it in testing.

render_layout() fast path in agent-state (lines 227-237) is dead code on macOS: case $cmd in claude) never matches because tmux pane_current_command reports claude's version string (e.g. 2.1.96), not 'claude'. detect_agent fallback handles everything correctly including prometheus promotion. Recommendation: delete fast path to remove duplication. Pending user approval as of 2026-04-09.

Skill analytics now use a PostToolUse hook (shared/steez/hooks/skill-analytics.sh) instead of inline preamble telemetry. The hook fires on every Skill tool invocation and writes to ~/.steez/analytics/skill-usage.jsonl. Schema: skill, ts, epoch, sid, repo, branch, agent, args. The installer creates the symlink at ~/.claude/hooks/steez-skill-analytics.sh and the doctor validates it. settings.json registration is manual — the installer prints the snippet if missing. telemetry.md is deleted, tiers.json no longer references telemetry, and no SKILL.md has inline telemetry code.

steez-agent-history (shared/steez/bin/steez-agent-history): bash wrapper + embedded python3 parser for Claude Code and Codex CLI JSONL transcripts. Three modes: --last, --blocked, --history N. Claude uses delta pattern (multiple JSONL lines per message.id). Codex uses event_msg/task_complete for turn boundaries. tail -500 with fallback to tail -2000 for performance. All error output is JSON via python3 json.dumps (not printf). Implemented in steez-j56.1, registration deferred to steez-j56.2.

Adversarial plan verification pattern: run prometheus-verification against the plan BEFORE decomposing into beads. The verifier checks plan claims against real data (transcripts, code, env vars). Two passes caught 4 issues in the steez-agent-history plan that would have become bugs. Key catches: fabricated env var (STEEZ_PANE), incomplete files-to-modify (setup.go, doctor.go, tests), wrong field name (questions[] not question). Each pass finds things the previous missed.

Deleting a steez skill touches FIVE sources of truth, not three: (1) skills/<name>/SKILL.md and the directory itself, (2) skills.json in two stanzas — the top-level skills map AND the matching category array under categories.X.skills (and shared_infra.bin if the skill ships a helper script), (3) ~/.steez/installed.json runtime registry on installed machines (not in repo, but steez doctor will flag a missing symlink until removed), (4) CLAUDE.md dependency map at the bottom — agent-state/agent-history/agent-send 'Used by:' lines list skill consumers by name, (5) README.md in THREE places — the slash-command table row at line ~73, the categories table around line ~123, AND the headline 'N-specialist' count at line 3. Also internal/installer/integration_test.go uses one skill name as a test fixture for TestIntegration_DoctorAfterInstall — if you delete the fixture skill, swap to a sibling. Pre-deletion blast-radius grep must include the slash-command form '/<name>' AND the bare identifier — README uses slash-command convention which the bare identifier grep misses entirely. Precedent commit 9fc4aa1 'docs: purge stale skill references from README and ARCHITECTURE' establishes README hygiene as in-scope for skill deletion work. Verified end-to-end during steez-d3b (delete tmux skill) — first pass missed README, verifier caught it, fixed in 3dc2e68.

steez agents/ directory created for subagent definitions. Agents are symlinked from repo to ~/.claude/agents/. Pattern: agents/steez-{name}.md in repo, symlink at ~/.claude/agents/steez-{name}.md. First two: steez-jira, steez-reminders. Installer does not yet manage agent symlinks — currently manual.

agent-history --all implemented (95e179c): scans all agent panes via agent-state --all --json, self-invokes per pane to reuse the existing single-pane parser, returns JSON array tagged with pane+name. Works with --last/--blocked/--history N. Incompatible with --agent override or explicit target. Uses 0x1f unit-separator delimited records internally.

agent-state --layout exists: renders ASCII box diagrams of tmux pane splits with agent state overlay. Uses tmux pane geometry + snap() for separator gaps. Behind --layout flag, not wired into any workflow. Might be useful for agent-spawn post-spawn reports someday.

Jira auth uses api_token (not OAuth). OAuth lacks Forms API scope (401 on /jira/forms/cloud/ endpoint). API token has no scope limitations. Config at ~/.config/acli/jira_config.yaml. Token stored in macOS Keychain under service 'acli'. All three Jira scripts (acli, jira_worklog.py, jira_forms.py) share the same Keychain credential.

steez skill-as-router pattern validated 2026-04-05. context:fork eliminates dedicated agents but loses hook-based telemetry (PostToolUse Skill never fires for forked skills). Current pattern (skill routes to named agent) is correct for observable skills. Keep dedicated agents for reminders/jira. context:fork only for skills that don't need per-skill analytics.

tmux pane geometry excludes 1-char inter-pane separator. Pane at left=0 width=88 and pane at left=89 width=88 have a 1-char gap at position 88. The snap() function in agent-state merges boundaries that are 1 apart to handle this.

tmux splits are per-pane, not per-column. Splitting one cell of a vertical stack horizontally only affects that cell, not the full column height. To create a proper multi-column grid, create all columns first (horizontal splits), then stack vertically within each column. Predefined layout recipes per agent count are more reliable than dynamic transitions.

acli jira project list requires --limit or --paginate flag. Without it: 'at least one of the flags in the group [recent limit paginate] is required'. JQL parse errors can mask auth failures — test with non-JQL command (acli jira project list --limit 3) to isolate.

bun test in browse (bun 1.3.5): passing a DIRECTORY arg silently matches zero files and exits 0. All three package.json scripts (test, test:core, test:ns) are broken this way — 'bun test v1.3.5' is the ONLY output. Use explicit file args OR cd into the test dir. This was the real invisibility mechanism behind the steez-rmb test-path drift sitting broken for months — not non-TTY output suppression. Single explicit file args work correctly (exit 1 on fail, full error trace).
heredoc vs pipe stdin conflict: python3 <<'EOF' steals stdin from pipe. Use python3 -c "$(cat <<'EOF' ... EOF)" to deliver code via -c and keep stdin free for piped data.

steez install all migrates old steez-prefixed skill symlinks to prefix-free names. The code in install.go:203-223 handles both: creating new prefix-free symlinks and removing old steez-* ones. If symlinks look stale after a code change, rebuild the binary (make install) and re-run steez install all. No code change needed — just a stale binary.

context:fork skills bypass ALL Skill tool hooks (PreToolUse, PostToolUse). SubagentStop fires but only carries agent_type (e.g. general-purpose), not skill name. Skills needing hook-based analytics (reminders, jira) must keep the skill-as-router + dedicated agent pattern. context:fork is viable for skills without telemetry needs. Tested empirically 2026-04-05.

agent-spawn skill (skills/agent-spawn/) uses agent-state for readiness detection in spawn.sh. The poll loop calls agent-state <pane> and parses JSON for state=working or state=idle. Works for prometheus and claude. Codex state detection is currently broken (separate bead). Default model is prometheus. Launch commands: prometheus (bare), claude --dangerously-skip-permissions, codex --dangerously-bypass-approvals-and-sandbox.
